title: mac.setup.sh: remove lm-studio (redundant with ollama)
description:
## Context
Earlier this session, `lm-studio` was added to `mac.setup.sh` brew install line (dotfiles-y3q, commit 32c3168). On reconsideration, it's redundant with the already-installed `ollama` for this fleet's actual use case:

- pico is headless → LM Studio's GUI is dead weight
- Workflow is agentic loops + scripting → Ollama's OpenAI-compat API is the right primitive
- MLX backend advantage closed when Ollama 0.19 added MLX support

Only honest case for LM Studio would be on metis (workstation) for occasional GUI-based Hugging Face model browsing — a workflow preference, not a capability gap. User opted to skip for now to keep the install footprint lean.

## Task
Remove `lm-studio` from `mac.setup.sh` brew install line.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] `lm-studio` no longer in `mac.setup.sh` brew line
- [x] Other items in the line unchanged

title: mac.setup.sh: note Tailscale variant trade-off (headless vs GUI on macOS)
description:
## Context
Phase 1 testing surfaced a macOS Tailscale architectural constraint we missed during /spec + /scrutinize: you can't have both Tailscale SSH server AND MagicDNS on the same macOS install. The brew formula (CLI/headless) gives the server but runs in userspace networking (no DNS). All GUI variants (App Store, "Standalone" download, brew cask) give DNS via Network Extension but are sandboxed and refuse to run Tailscale SSH server.

`mac.setup.sh` installs the formula via brew (correct for headless servers like pico). The script doesn't document the trade-off, so anyone reading it for a workstation Mac (metis-style) would get the wrong variant for that role.

## Task
1. Add a comment block above the zig-zone bring-up section in `mac.setup.sh` explaining:
   - This script installs the FORMULA (headless tailscaled)
   - Right for HEADLESS SERVERS (Tailscale SSH server works)
   - For WORKSTATIONS: swap to cask post-install with explicit commands (including the `sudo rm -f /usr/local/bin/tailscale*` step for cleaning up the formula's symlinks)
   - Reference to runbook gotcha #12 for the full rationale
2. Add a note at the END of the script reminding users about the `/etc/hosts` shim for outbound MagicDNS

## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] Comment block above zig-zone section explains variant trade-off
- [x] Explicit cask-swap commands for workstation use case
- [x] Reference to runbook gotcha #12
- [x] /etc/hosts shim note at end of script
- [x] No behavior change for the headless-server use case (pico's bring-up still works as-is)

title: zig-zone: zig (work laptop) deferred from tailnet at user's discretion
description:
## Context
zig-zone spec (dotfiles-phe) named 5 devices: zig-computer, pico, metis, zig (work laptop), iPhone. OQ-03 decided "zig joins as autogroup:member" — but the actual JOIN was deferred at Phase 1 by the user, who wanted to check work-laptop-policy implications first before installing Tailscale on a work-managed device.

## State as of 2026-05-23
- Tailnet: zig-zone, 4 of 5 devices joined (zig-computer, pico, metis, iPhone)
- zig (work laptop): no Tailscale install yet. SSH from zig to zig-computer continues to work via public port 22 with SSH key (LIMITed sshd + fail2ban — current break-glass posture)
- ACL is in place and would accept zig as autogroup:member when she joins (no ACL change needed for her addition)

## What's needed when zig joins
1. Check work device-management policy allows Tailscale install (the actual OQ-03 verification)
2. `brew install tailscale && sudo brew services start tailscale && sudo tailscale up` — auth as same Tailscale account
3. Verify in `tailscale status` from any other device
4. No tag (stays untagged → autogroup:member)
5. No `--ssh` flag (client only)
6. Test `tailscale ssh ubuntu@zig-computer.zig-zone.ts.net` from zig

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Work-laptop policy verified to allow Tailscale (OQ-03 closure)
- [ ] zig joined to zig-zone tailnet as autogroup:member
- [ ] Tailscale SSH from zig to zig-computer + pico confirmed working
- [ ] Public port 22 SSH from zig still works as break-glass

title: a1111: inpaint NaN crash on MPS — missing --upcast-attn
description:
## Context

A1111 install on pico (dotfiles-rp0) shipped without enabling the
"Upcast cross attention layer to float32" setting. User attempted SDXL
inpainting immediately after install and hit:

```
NansException: A tensor with NaNs was produced in Unet. This could be
either because there's not enough precision to represent the picture,
or because your video card does not support half type. Try setting the
"Upcast cross attention layer to float32" option in Settings > Stable
Diffusion or using the --no-half commandline argument to fix this.
Time taken: 13.8 sec.
```

Known MPS issue with SDXL inpainting — the cross-attention layer
underflows to NaN at fp16 precision.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Fresh A1111 install on Apple Silicon via mac.setup.sh (as shipped
   in rp0)
2. Load SDXL base 1.0 model
3. Open Img2Img tab → Inpaint sub-tab
4. Upload any image, draw a mask, run generation
5. After ~14 seconds, NansException raised; no output produced

## Fix

The error message itself recommends two options: (a) Settings > Stable
Diffusion > "Upcast cross attention layer to float32" toggle, or
(b) `--no-half` CLI arg. The Settings toggle is targeted (~minor
perf hit on the cross-attention layer only); --no-half is heavy
(doubles model memory).

A1111 1.10 has NO CLI flag for the Settings toggle — only the toggle
itself, which lives in `config.json` as `upcast_attn: true`. Created
`~/dotfiles/a1111/config.json` with the toggle pre-set; mac.setup.sh
installs it on fresh-Mac rebuild (only if config.json doesn't already
exist — preserves UI-saved settings on re-runs). Synced to pico's live
config.json + kickstarted LaunchAgent.

(Initial attempt added `--upcast-attn` to COMMANDLINE_ARGS; A1111
rejected it as unrecognized. Reverted to config.json-based fix.)

## Regression guard

- **Structural**: `~/dotfiles/a1111/config.json` is the seed file
  mac.setup.sh installs on fresh-Mac bootstrap. Future installs get
  the upcast_attn toggle automatically.
- **Documentation**: README.md in `~/dotfiles/a1111/` lists config.json
  in the files table with the reason.
- **Code comment**: `webui-user.sh` notes that the Settings toggle (no
  CLI flag) is needed for SDXL inpainting on MPS and points at
  `a1111/config.json`.

## Acceptance criteria

- [x] config.json seeded with upcast_attn=true in dotfiles
- [x] mac.setup.sh installs the seed (idempotent: only when missing)
- [x] Pico's live config.json updated
- [x] LaunchAgent kickstarted; A1111 boots cleanly with new config
- [x] README documents the file + reason
- [ ] User verifies SDXL inpainting now succeeds (their re-test confirms)

title: explore: SS14-on-pico via TPROXY or GRE — preserve source IPs + VPS-IP public face
description:
## Context

zig-zone Phase 4 (bead `dotfiles-hdo`) attempted moving SS14 game server +
watchdog to pico via nginx `stream{}` UDP-proxy on zig-computer. Rolled
back to zig-computer 2026-05-24 because nginx UDP proxy collapses all
client source IPs into the proxy's own IP — breaks SS14's moderation
subsystem (ban_address, GeoIP, ipintel_cache, admin logs all become
useless when every player's RemoteEndPoint is nginx's IP). Architecturally
definitive, regardless of any test-time perf symptoms.

**ARM64 build artifacts preserved on pico** for whenever this future
migration runs:
- `/Users/pico/ss14-watchdog/` — full instance layout, darwin-arm64
  binaries (Robust.Server + Content.Server + SS14.Watchdog), patched
  `config.toml` (pg_host=localhost, Loki=localhost, UpdateType=Dummy),
  Resources/ + Content.Client.zip preserved
- `~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.ss14-watchdog.plist` — on disk, unloaded
- `~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.ss14-replay-rotate.plist` — on disk, unloaded
- Build recipe: `dotnet publish Content.Server -c Release -r osx-arm64
  --self-contained -p:FullRelease=True` + same for SS14.Watchdog

## Goal

**Move SS14 to pico while keeping (a) source IPs preserved for moderation
and (b) zig-computer's IP as the only publicly-known address.** Both
constraints together rule out home-router DNAT (would expose home WAN IP).

Lack of documented production SS14 operator running TPROXY / GRE is NOT a
reason to skip — SS14 is a niche game, the absence is an absence of
evidence, not evidence of absence. Exhaust technical options before
accepting compromise.

## The trade-off that killed the DNAT plan (2026-05-24)

The original 52c plan was home-router DNAT (DDNS hostname resolves to home
WAN IP → router forwards UDP/TCP :1212 to pico LAN IP). User flagged that
this exposes the home public IP to anyone who reads the SS14 status
endpoint or inspects a launcher connection, which conflicts with the
zig-zone premise of "VPS is the only public face." Pivot to architectures
that route game traffic through zig-computer while preserving source IPs.

## Approaches to evaluate

### Approach A — TPROXY (nginx transparent UDP proxy on zig-computer)

Linux kernel `IP_TRANSPARENT` socket option + netfilter rules + nginx
stream module's `proxy_bind $remote_addr transparent;`. Packets reach pico
with the real client IP intact in `RemoteEndPoint`.

Engineering requirements:
- nginx workers run with `CAP_NET_ADMIN` + `CAP_NET_RAW`
- iptables MARK rule on the inbound :1212 traffic
- Policy routing rule: marked packets use a custom routing table that
  routes via lo (loopback)
- **Asymmetric routing problem**: pico's response packets default-route
  out pico's own LAN/WAN, NOT back through zig-computer. Client sees
  responses from a different src IP → connection breaks. Fix options:
  - (a) Route pico's responses back through zig-computer via Tailscale +
    a custom route on pico for the public-IP /32
  - (b) Source-NAT on pico's responses so they appear to come from
    zig-computer's IP (defeats the point — we WANT pico to know the
    real client; this just hides nginx's spoof from the client's POV)
  - (c) Run TPROXY in "tee" mode — only one direction is transparent;
    other direction goes through nginx normally

The Tailscale layer adds wrinkles — Tailscale already does its own
src-IP-rewriting on the wireguard tunnel; layering TPROXY on top requires
making sure the marked packets aren't double-translated.

### Approach B — GRE / IP-in-IP tunnel between zig-computer and pico

Kernel tunnel that encapsulates entire IP packets. From pico's perspective,
a virtual interface (`gif0` on macOS) appears to be on zig-computer's
network. Bind Robust.Server to that interface's IP and the public address
"reaches" pico transparently.

Engineering requirements:
- Linux side: `ip tunnel add gre0 mode gre remote <pico-IP> local
  <zig-IP>` (or `ip6gre` for IPv6)
- macOS side: `gif0` interface config via `ifconfig gif0 create
  tunnel <pico-IP> <zig-IP>` — supported on macOS but underdocumented
- Routing on each end: public :1212 traffic gets routed through the
  tunnel instead of direct
- Same asymmetric-routing concern — pico's response must traverse the
  tunnel back, not its default route
- Tailscale-on-top: adding GRE alongside Tailscale on the same hosts
  is redundant tunneling. Could replace Tailscale for this one flow,
  or carry GRE *inside* the tailnet (GRE-over-WireGuard).

### Approach C (escalation if A+B both fail) — fork Robust.Server with a
nginx-mapping sidecar

nginx stream maintains per-client upstream-socket mapping (already does
this for UDP). Expose that mapping over a UDS to a small sidecar; patch
Robust.Server to consult the sidecar on connect, override
RemoteEndPoint.Address with the real client IP. Fork-and-PR scope; an
engineering project that touches the community engine but doesn't require
upstream merge if we self-host.

## Testing methodology

For each approach:
1. Set up a minimal test bench — nginx stream config on zig-computer +
   tcpdump on pico for `:1212`
2. Smoke test with a synthetic UDP client from a third tailnet device
   (verify pico's tcpdump shows the third device's IP, not zig-computer's)
3. Real-player test — connect with the SS14 client; `psql -c "SELECT
   address FROM ban_address"` after a test ban confirms the real client
   IP landed in the table
4. Stability test — sustained 30-min play session, monitor for
   reconnect events or asymmetric-routing-induced drops
5. Failover test — kill nginx mid-session, ensure clean recovery on restart

If A or B works: write up the config, archive in zig-zone runbook as a
new phase, plan the actual SS14 migration as a separate bead.

If neither A nor B works after honest investigation: document why in the
runbook, then escalate to approach C OR accept SS14-stays-on-zig-computer
as the long-term answer.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] TPROXY (Approach A) tested end-to-end: synthetic client + real
      player + ban_address verification. PASS or FAIL recorded with
      tcpdump evidence + nginx config snapshot.
- [ ] GRE (Approach B) tested end-to-end: same protocol. PASS or FAIL
      recorded with tunnel config snapshot from both ends + asymmetric
      routing handling documented.
- [ ] If either passes: SS14 migration plan filed as separate
      implementation bead (typed `task`), with the working approach
      referenced.
- [ ] If both fail: failure modes documented in zig-zone runbook (new
      gotcha), Approach C feasibility note added.
- [ ] Outcome reflected in zig-zone runbook Phase 4 section (either flip
      to SHIPPED with the working approach, or note exploration result).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] DDNS hostname registered + update cron running on pico (or router)
 - [ ] Pico has static DHCP lease on home LAN
 - [ ] Home router DNAT rule forwards external :1212 UDP+TCP → pico LAN IP
 - [ ] Pico Robust.Server config.toml has status.connectaddress = udp://<ddns-hostname>:1212
 - [ ] SS14 watchdog + replay-rotate disabled on zig-computer
 - [ ] SS14 watchdog + replay-rotate LaunchAgents loaded on pico
 - [ ] install-timers.sh updated to re-add ss14-replay-rotate (8th entry)
 - [ ] Real player connects + ban_address SELECT shows real IPs (not nginx's)
 - [ ] /status players count works correctly (gotcha #22 cvar already applied)
 - [ ] No regression in other zig-zone services (verify Phase 3 routes still 200)
 - [ ] Runbook updated: Phase 4 outcome flipped to SHIPPED with the DNAT pattern documented
notes:
## Pivot — 2026-05-24 (post-offboard)

Original plan (home-router DNAT) rejected by user when they surfaced the
hidden cost: DDNS resolves to home WAN IP, which becomes the publicly-
known game IP. Conflicts with the zig-zone premise that zig-computer's
IP is the only public face. Updated description + retitled bead;
type flipped task → study because this is now exploration-first.

User explicitly: 'just bc theres no documented servers out there that
have done this doesnt mean we cant or that we should be discouraged
(this is a niche game). i'd rather exhaust our options and rigorously
attempt them before giving up or making compromises'.

TPROXY (Approach A) and GRE (Approach B) are the two paths to test.


## Phase 1 + 2 — Research and recommendation (2026-05-24, exploration subagent)

### Findings

**TPROXY (Approach A) — significantly harder than initial framing.**
nginx `proxy_bind $remote_addr transparent` solves the OUTBOUND half
(forwarding to pico with spoofed src=client). The RETURN half on a
remote backend requires per-F5/NGINX docs either (a) flipping pico's
default route to zig-computer (breaks pico outbound), (b) `tc nat
egress` on backend (doesn't exist on macOS — pfctl would be the rough
equivalent, uncharted), or (c) per-client routes on pico that don't
scale (one route per connected player). No clean, scalable fix exists
for the macOS-backend variant.

**GRE / IP-in-IP (Approach B) — viable, with one macOS caveat.**
macOS gif(4) supports IP-in-IP ONLY, not GRE. So the test is actually
"IP-in-IP", which Linux supports via `ip tunnel mode ipip`. The
asymmetric routing problem dissolves because the tunnel IS the return
path. Recommended outer encapsulation: carry the tunnel inside Tailscale
(outer src/dst = tailnet IPs) — sidesteps potential ISP filtering of
protocol-4 packets on the public path, and reuses the existing trust /
encryption envelope. Per-client routing on pico is still required for
the asymmetric-routing fix, but only ONE route is needed if the test
sends from a known IP (and per-flow routes can be scripted later for
production via pf tables).

### Recommendation: START WITH GRE (Approach B, sub-bead dotfiles-jjd)

Reasoning:
1. **Simpler PASS/FAIL signal**: a working tunnel either ferries
   packets with source IPs intact, or it doesn't. No "works for one
   direction but breaks the return path" ambiguity.
2. **Lower production risk**: tunnel work is entirely on a NEW
   interface (gre0 / gif0); no iptables changes on the production
   inbound path; nginx changes are a NEW config file at port :11212
   that doesn't touch the live :1212 SS14 stream.
3. **Cleaner rollback**: `ip tunnel del`, `ifconfig gif0 -tunnel`,
   `rm /etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-gre-test.conf`, single
   `nginx -s reload`. Each step is a single command.
4. **Documentation**: while no SS14 operator runs this in production,
   the IP-in-IP tunnel pattern is decades-old and well-documented for
   Linux↔BSD interop (RFC 4213). Cisco / Juniper have shipped IPIP-
   over-VPN deployments for years. The novelty here is wiring nginx
   on top, not the tunnel itself.

### What test SHOULD NOT be run unless GRE fails

TPROXY-to-macOS-remote-backend (sub-bead dotfiles-r8k). The
asymmetric-return-path problem is the cliff; without a clear macOS
pfctl recipe (which would itself be a multi-day investigation), the
test would hit the same wall the NGINX/F5 blog calls out. Defer
TPROXY testing until either (a) GRE fails AND (b) someone surfaces
a confirmed pfctl recipe for stateless source rewriting on macOS UDP.

### Next action (Phase 3 — execute GRE test plan)

See dotfiles-jjd description for the full step-by-step. Subagent will
execute through Step 4b (synthetic UDP-echo with source preservation)
and STOP before Step 5 (real-SS14-client) — that requires orchestrator
confirmation because it touches the production game server's port
namespace (even at :11212, the Robust.Server process invocation is a
production-class step).


## Phase 3 — execution result (2026-05-24, GRE/IPIP track)

**Outcome: GRE/IPIP-over-Tailscale path BLOCKED by upstream
limitation. Synthetic UDP test never reached.**

Test got through Step 2 (tunnel up on both ends, ifconfig shows clean
config) and FAILED at Step 2.5 (connectivity verification, plain ping
across the tunnel-inner /30). 100% packet loss. Root-caused via
tcpdump:

- pico's utun5 (Tailscale interface) shows proto-4 (ipencap) packets
  leaving correctly
- zig-computer's tailscale0 receives ZERO proto-4 packets
- Tailscale's WireGuard implementation drops non-TCP/UDP/ICMP IP
  protocols on the way through. Confirmed by upstream issue
  tailscale/tailscale#14006 ("IPIP is not working over tailscale0"),
  open since 2024-11-02, unresolved

### Implications

The "carry the tunnel inside Tailscale" architecture (what made the
GRE approach cleanly compatible with the zig-zone premise of "VPS
public IP is the only exposed address") is not actually realizable.
The two fallback options both fail the brief:

- **Carry the tunnel over the public IPs**: outer dst becomes pico's
  home WAN IP. Anyone running tcpdump anywhere on the path from
  zig-computer to pico sees the home WAN IP. Same exposure problem as
  the DNAT plan that pivoted us here.
- **Add a second cloud relay (e.g., another VPS as middle hop)**:
  defeats the simplification — we already HAVE a cloud box
  (zig-computer); adding another to relay tunnels around Tailscale's
  protocol restriction is escalation beyond "test in an afternoon."

### TPROXY (dotfiles-r8k) — STILL NOT TESTED, but Phase 1 research
already documented why it doesn't work for the macOS-backend variant:
the F5/NGINX canonical fix requires either flipping pico's default
route (breaks Ollama, brew, etc.) or `tc nat egress` on the backend
(doesn't exist on macOS — would need an uncharted pfctl recipe). The
TPROXY test's --description has the full reasoning.

### Net result for parent 52c

**Both Approach A (TPROXY) and Approach B (GRE/IPIP) are blocked by
real-world constraints that the original spec didn't account for**:

- TPROXY: the F5 docs assume Linux backends with `tc`; macOS doesn't
  have it. The pfctl-based rewrite is a research project, not a test.
- GRE/IPIP: cleanly works only over the public path, which leaks
  pico's home WAN IP — the very constraint we built this exploration
  to avoid.

**Escalation paths for orchestrator + user to decide between**:

1. **Approach C from the original spec** (fork Robust.Server with a
   nginx-mapping sidecar): biggest engineering scope but most likely
   to actually deliver the goal. Bead would need to be a `-t task` of
   its own with subtasks for the sidecar, the patch, the testing.

2. **Tailscale subnet routing + advertise pico's gif0 inner /30**: if
   we install another box (e.g., a Raspberry Pi on pico's home LAN)
   that DOES carry the tunnel over the public IP to zig-computer's
   public IP, then advertises pico's /32 over Tailscale, we get the
   topology we want without Tailscale carrying ipip. This is a
   physical-infra purchase, not a software change.

3. **Accept the architectural-definitive verdict**: SS14 stays on
   zig-computer permanently. Document the failed exploration as a
   gotcha in the zig-zone runbook (new entry citing tailscale issue
   #14006). Close 52c, jjd, and r8k as RESOLVED-not-viable.

4. **Wait-and-watch**: file an upstream comment on tailscale#14006
   asking for IPIP support, revisit if/when it lands. Don't expect
   action.

Recommendation: **option 3 (accept and document)** is the highest-
signal-per-effort move. Option 1 is the most powerful but biggest
investment. Options 2 and 4 are speculative.

### Beads to update

- This bead 52c: now ready for user decision on escalation path
- dotfiles-jjd: --notes block updated with full Phase 3 results,
  rollback verified, ready to close as "GRE/IPIP via Tailscale not
  viable; alternative paths require infra investment beyond scope"
- dotfiles-r8k: --description has the Phase 1 reasoning for why
  TPROXY-to-macOS-backend is the wrong tool; no execution attempted
  (zero risk run); ready to close as "not viable for this topology"

### Production safety throughout

SS14 :1212 listeners (4 of them, UDP+TCP v4+v6) were verified live
before, during, and after the test. Zero touches to
`/etc/nginx/sites-available/ss14.zig.computer.conf`. No iptables
rules added (the test plan included some but we never reached the
step that would add them). No UFW changes. All rollback verified
against pre-test snapshots in /tmp/*.pretest.1779647689.

title: hooks: harden pre-commit-checks.sh — worktree-push guard + Bead-trailer block
description:
## Context
Two git-discipline rules are text-only today. (1) /commit's "worktree
exception" — subagents don't push; the orchestrator merges + pushes —
sits as a paragraph below a `git push` heading, easy to skim past; a
worktree push leaves stale worktree-agent-* remote branches. (2)
pre-commit-checks.sh only WARNS on a missing `Bead:` trailer (exit 0) —
an invisible warning under token pressure; the gap surfaces later as
`br orphans`.

## Task
Harden pre-commit-checks.sh (PreToolUse:Bash):
1. Block `git push` when cwd is inside .claude/worktrees/agent-*.
2. Upgrade the missing-Bead-trailer check from warn to block, exempting
   meta-commits (:card_file_box: / :broom: / :dollar: gitmoji — the
   bead-state / triage / offboard / cost commits that reference beads
   in the subject, not a trailer).
Preserve the git-add-A block, bead-sync, and staged-lint behaviour.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] git push from a worktree cwd blocked; from project root, allowed
- [ ] commit with no Bead: trailer blocked
- [ ] :card_file_box: / :broom: / :dollar: meta-commits exempt
- [ ] git-add-A block + bead-sync + staged-lint preserved
- [ ] new test-pre-commit-checks.sh green
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] worktree-push guard blocks git push from a worktree cwd
 - [ ] missing Bead: trailer blocks; meta-commits (:card_file_box:/:broom:/:dollar:) exempt
 - [ ] git-add-A block + staged-lint preserved; new test green

title: mac.setup.sh: fix xtools-select typo blocking fresh-Mac bootstrap
description:
## Problem
`mac.setup.sh` line 1 reads `xtools-select --install` (typo — `xtools` instead of `xcode`). On a fresh Mac with no Xcode Command Line Tools installed (the pico bootstrap scenario), this fails silently as `command not found`, then line 3 attempts to install Homebrew, which requires a compiler from Xcode CLT, so Homebrew install also fails.

## Source of report
Surfaced by /scrutinize agent reviewing the zig-zone spec (`dotfiles-phe`), 2026-05-23. Confirmed by Read of `/home/ubuntu/dotfiles/mac.setup.sh:1`.

## Steps to Reproduce
On a fresh macOS install with no Xcode CLI tools (`xcode-select -p` returns "command not found"):
1. `git clone https://github.com/azigler/dotfiles ~/dotfiles`
2. `bash ~/dotfiles/mac.setup.sh`
3. Observe: line 1 errors `xtools-select: command not found`, line 3 (Homebrew install) then errors because Homebrew's install script requires a compiler from Xcode CLT.
Expected: `xcode-select --install` opens the CLT install dialog, blocks until installed, then Homebrew install proceeds.

## Fix
Correct `xtools-select` → `xcode-select`.

## Why this is a regression guard
This is a one-character typo in a script that hasn't run end-to-end on a fresh Mac since the typo landed. No test catches it because the existing Mac (the one editing this file) already has CLT installed and skips that line's side effect. New regression guard: a shellcheck-style lint test that verifies known commands exist (we don't have one and adding it is overkill for this single fix), OR a manual "run setup.sh in a fresh macOS VM" rehearsal as part of pico bootstrap. The latter is implicitly part of the zig-zone spec's Prerequisites section.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] `mac.setup.sh:1` reads `xcode-select --install`
- [x] No other occurrences of `xtools-select` in the dotfiles repo

title: vs14-mapserver: ARM64 native build for pico (qemu signal 6 on x64 image)
description:
## Context
Phase 3 of zig-zone migration hit ARM64 wall on pico for `vs14-mapserver` container. SS14 docs note "Apple Silicon (ARM64) Mac users: Some older codebases will only work with the x64 .NET and not the ARM64 one." User wanted methodical investigation before falling back to keeping mapserver on zig-computer.

## Symptom
Container `vs14-mapserver` enters startup, exits with `qemu: uncaught target signal 6 (Aborted) - core dumped` after running EF Core startup migration helper. `docker image inspect vs14-mapserver:latest --format '{{.Architecture}}'` returns `amd64` despite Colima running aarch64. Indicates qemu emulation in use.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. On pico (M1 Max), with Colima running aarch64 (`colima list` shows `aarch64`)
2. `cd /Users/pico/vacation-station-14/ops/observability && docker-compose up -d mapserver`
3. `docker logs vs14-mapserver` — see qemu signal 6 (Aborted) on EF Core init
4. `docker image inspect vs14-mapserver:latest --format '{{.Architecture}}'` reports `amd64`, not `arm64`

## Setup
- Pico: Mac Studio M1 Max, macOS 14.4, Colima 4cpu/8GiB/60GiB aarch64
- Dockerfile: external/mapserver/Dockerfile, FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:9.0 (multi-arch image, no platform override)
- csproj: external/mapserver/SS14.MapServer/SS14.MapServer.csproj, TargetFramework=net9.0, NO RuntimeIdentifier locked

## Investigation log
### Attempt 1: DOCKER_DEFAULT_PLATFORM=linux/arm64 docker-compose build --no-cache mapserver
Result: still amd64. docker-compose silently picked amd64 manifest.

### Attempt 2: docker build --platform linux/arm64 --no-cache -t vs14-mapserver:arm64-test .
Result: SUCCESS. Native ARM64 build. `Setting up libfreetype6:arm64` in apt-get logs confirms arm64 package install. Final image arch reports arm64.

### Resolution — SHIPPED 2026-05-24
Build with bare `docker build --platform linux/arm64 ...` (bypassing compose), tag as :latest:
```bash
docker build --platform linux/arm64 --no-cache -t vs14-mapserver:arm64-test .
docker tag vs14-mapserver:arm64-test vs14-mapserver:latest
docker-compose up -d --force-recreate mapserver
```
Runs cleanly. No qemu, no signal 6. Same fix applied to ss14-admin (.NET 10 SDK), also builds ARM64 successfully.

The SS14 docs's "x64-only codebases" warning targets older RobustToolbox dependencies; mapserver's .csproj has no native dep that requires x64. The bug was docker-compose's build-platform handling, not the source code.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Root cause of amd64 build identified (Dockerfile FROM selector, Colima default, docker-compose ignore, or csproj dep)
 - [ ] Either: ARM64 image builds + runs successfully on pico, OR fallback documented (mapserver stays on zig-computer for Phase 3, future spec covers ARM64 port)
 - [ ] Bead notes capture each attempt with command + result + diagnosis
notes: Validates SS14 docs note on Apple Silicon compatibility — issue was build invocation, not codebase. `docker build --platform linux/arm64` reliably; `docker-compose build` with DOCKER_DEFAULT_PLATFORM env doesn't propagate consistently.

title: spec: SS14-on-pico via Approach C2 — nginx + wrapper-pair + minimal Robust.Server patches
description:
## 1. Overview

### 1.1 Sources

This spec is informed by:

- Parent bead `dotfiles-52c` (explore: SS14-on-pico via TPROXY or GRE) — full rationale for why DNAT was rejected (exposes home WAN IP) and why TPROXY (`dotfiles-r8k`) and GRE/IPIP-over-Tailscale (`dotfiles-jjd`) were ruled out.
- The zig-zone runbook at `~/explore/.claude/skills/zig-zone/SKILL.md` — 22 production gotchas, Phase 4 ATTEMPTED+ROLLED-BACK history, Colima/Tailscale/macOS networking quirks, the `/etc/hosts` shim for headless tailscaled on pico, the established convention that LaunchAgents live at `~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.<service>.plist`.
- The `vacation-station-14` source tree at `~/vacation-station-14/` (community fork; `git log -1` shows `b8c8c54d48`). Specific moderation-path locations grepped and confirmed (see Section 2).
- The Robust.Server vendored copy at `~/vacation-station-14/RobustToolbox/Robust.Server/` and `Robust.Shared/Network/` — confirms `INetChannel.RemoteEndPoint` is the universal IP-source; confirms Lidgren has zero PROXY-protocol parsing.
- nginx 1.28.0 on zig-computer (`apt-cache policy nginx`) built `--with-stream=dynamic --with-stream_realip_module` — stream module + PROXY protocol support both present.
- Phase 4 build artifacts on pico (per `dotfiles-52c`): `/Users/pico/ss14-watchdog/` instance dir, darwin-arm64 binaries preserved, two LaunchAgent plists on disk but unloaded.

### 1.2 Goal

Move SS14 (game server + watchdog + replay-rotate) from zig-computer to pico (Mac Studio on the tailnet) **while preserving real client source IPs** in every SS14 moderation-subsystem code path AND keeping zig-computer's IP `51.81.33.136` as the only publicly-known address.

### 1.3 Approach (C2) in one paragraph

nginx `stream{}` on zig-computer accepts UDP+TCP on public `:1212`, then forwards each connection to a **wrapper daemon** on pico over the tailnet, with a PROXY-protocol header prepended to convey the real client IP+port (PROXY v1 text on UDP — per /check dotfiles-9cj empirical test of nginx 1.28; PROXY v2 binary on TCP per nginx default). The wrapper listens on pico's tailnet IP `:1212`, peels the PROXY header, forwards the stripped UDP/TCP payload to `127.0.0.1:1213` where Robust.Server binds. The wrapper maintains a 5-tuple → real-client-IP map and exposes it over a localhost Unix Domain Socket (UDS) at `/Users/pico/run/ss14-wrapper.sock`. Robust.Server is patched (in two places: the connect-time `NetConnectingArgs.IP` synthesis in `NetManager.ServerAuth.cs`, and the `NetChannel.RemoteEndPoint` property in `NetManager.NetChannel.cs`) to consult the wrapper's UDS map and override the apparent peer IP. All downstream moderation paths (`BanManager`, `ConnectionManager`, `IPIntel`, `ServerApi`, `PlayerLocator`, `AdminAlertIfSharedConnection`) read the corrected IP transparently because they all funnel through those two override points.

### 1.4 In scope

- nginx `stream{}` config on zig-computer with UDP+TCP `proxy_protocol on` + `proxy_pass` to pico's tailnet IP `:1212`.
- Wrapper daemon design + implementation (single binary, single config file).
- Two minimal patches to vendored Robust.Server (NetManager.ServerAuth.cs synthesis site + NetChannel.RemoteEndPoint property).
- The wrapper's `~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.ss14-wrapper.plist` (KeepAlive=true, follows zig-zone naming convention).
- Updates to `~/dotfiles/vs14/` plist templates for ss14-watchdog (port flip 1212 → 1213) and the new wrapper plist.
- zig-zone runbook update: Phase 4 transitions from "rolled back" to "SHIPPED via Approach C2" with a new section + 1–2 new gotchas.
- Cutover playbook on zig-computer (stop ss14-watchdog, reload nginx with stream block) + on pico (load LaunchAgents in the right order).

### 1.5 Out of scope

- Anything touching Lidgren-Network internals (no PROXY parsing INSIDE Lidgren — that's why C2 has a wrapper).
- Upstream-PR work to space-wizards/RobustToolbox (vacation-station-14 is a community fork already; the patches stay in our tree).
- The A1111 / Ollama / Phoenix / vs14-web / postgres / observability stack on pico — those are untouched. Only port allocations are coordinated (Section 4.5).
- vs14-mapserver port :44880 — this spec does NOT consume that. Wrapper allocates ports outside the existing taken set.
- IPv6 dual-stack handling beyond "IPv4 ships first; IPv6 is a separately-tracked follow-up." Robust.Server's default `net.bindto = "0.0.0.0,::"` means dual-stack matters eventually; first ship is IPv4-only with the IPv6 path documented as a follow-up bead.
- DDOS protection at the wrapper layer (relies on nginx upstream + tailnet ACL).

## 2. Current State / Baseline

### 2.1 SS14 runtime topology (post-Phase 4 rollback)

- Robust.Server + Content.Server + SS14.Watchdog all live on **zig-computer**, bound on `0.0.0.0:1212` (UDP + TCP, IPv4 + IPv6 — 4 listeners total per `ss -tunap | grep :1212`).
- Pico has **preserved** darwin-arm64 build artifacts under `/Users/pico/ss14-watchdog/` (instance laid out, binaries published, Resources/ + Content.Client.zip intact, `config.toml` already patched for `pg_host=localhost` + `loki=http://localhost:3100` + `UpdateType=Dummy`).
- LaunchAgents `~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.ss14-watchdog.plist` + `com.zig.ss14-replay-rotate.plist` exist on pico but are **unloaded**.
- Zig-computer has nginx 1.28.0 with the stream module installed (`/etc/nginx/nginx.conf` includes `stream { include /etc/nginx/streams-enabled/*; }`) but `/etc/nginx/streams-enabled/` is currently empty.

### 2.2 SS14 moderation code paths that consume `RemoteEndPoint.Address`

Grep results in `~/vacation-station-14/`:

**Content.Server (the fork's content layer):**
- `Content.Server/Connection/ConnectionManager.cs:115` — commented-out `HandleApproval`, reads `eventArgs.Connection.RemoteEndPoint.Address`.
- `Content.Server/Connection/ConnectionManager.cs:137` (LIVE) — `var addr = e.IP.Address` inside `NetMgrOnConnecting`; addr is then passed to `_db.AddConnectionLogAsync(...)` and `_db.GetBansAsync(...)` via `ShouldDeny` (line 214).
- `Content.Server/Connection/ConnectionManager.cs:184` — `var addr = newSession.Channel.RemoteEndPoint.Address` inside `AdminAlertIfSharedConnection`.
- `Content.Server/Connection/IPIntel/IPIntel.cs:110` — `var ip = e.IP.Address` inside `IsVpnOrProxy`; this drives `_db.GetIPIntelCache(ip)` (the `ipintel_cache` table the parent bead names).
- `Content.Server/Administration/Managers/BanManager.cs:75` — `netChannel.RemoteEndPoint.Address` inside `CachePlayerData` (role-ban lookup).
- `Content.Server/Administration/Managers/BanManager.cs:203` — `player.Channel.RemoteEndPoint.Address` inside `BanMatchesPlayer` (live ban-check).
- `Content.Server/Administration/PlayerLocator.cs:154` — `session.Channel.RemoteEndPoint.Address`.
- `Content.Server/Administration/PlayerPanelEui.cs:182` — `s.Channel.RemoteEndPoint.Address` for shared-connection count display.
- `Content.Server/Administration/ServerApi.cs:371` — `info.AddAddress(player.Channel.RemoteEndPoint.Address)` for the admin API response.
- `Content.Server/Administration/Managers/AdminManager.cs:505` — `var ep = player.Channel.RemoteEndPoint`.
- `Content.Server/Info/RulesManager.cs:32` — `IPAddress.IsLoopback(e.Channel.RemoteEndPoint.Address)` for the local-host bypass.

**Robust.Server / Robust.Shared (vendored engine, the override point):**
- `RobustToolbox/Robust.Shared/Network/INetChannel.cs:27` — interface contract `IPEndPoint RemoteEndPoint { get; }`.
- `RobustToolbox/Robust.Shared/Network/NetManager.NetChannel.cs:40` — implementation `public IPEndPoint RemoteEndPoint => _connection.RemoteEndPoint` (proxies through to Lidgren's `NetConnection`).
- `RobustToolbox/Robust.Shared/Network/NetManager.ServerAuth.cs:231` — `var endPoint = connection.RemoteEndPoint` then `new NetConnectingArgs(userData, endPoint, type)` — this is where `NetConnectingArgs.IP` is born. Override here = `e.IP.Address` in Content.Server gets the real client IP.

### 2.3 Network topology pieces

- Tailnet IPs at time of writing (per gotcha #17 / `/etc/hosts` shim): zig-computer `100.98.174.21`; pico `100.95.4.73` (was `100.72.47.4` during the GRE test 2026-05-24; tailnet IPs are stable per-device but check `tailscale ip -4` at install time).
- zig-computer public IP `51.81.33.136` is the only publicly-known game endpoint (DNS: `ss14.zig.computer`).
- Players currently dial `ss14.zig.computer:1212`. After C2, the dial target is the same — only the back-end moves.

### 2.4 Pico port usage inventory (must not collide)

Per the zig-zone runbook + dotfiles-52c context, pico already runs:

| Service | Bind |
|---|---|
| ssh | :22 |
| nginx (pico-side internal) | 0.0.0.0:8080 |
| Phoenix | tailnet-IP:6006 + tailnet-IP:4317 |
| Ollama | tailnet-IP:11434 |
| postgres | *:5432 |
| vs14-web | tailnet-IP:3300 |
| Colima docker workload | various 0.0.0.0 ports (mapserver, ss14-admin, prometheus, loki, grafana, cdn) |
| reef-router (when Phase 3 deferred bead lands) | tailnet-IP:7575 |
| A1111 | :7860 |
| (deferred from Phase 3) timers / cron — no ports |

**C2 allocations** (must not collide):
- Wrapper public-facing UDP+TCP listener: tailnet-IP:1212 (NEW — was free).
- Robust.Server internal UDP+TCP listener: `127.0.0.1:1213` (NEW — was free).
- Wrapper UDS for API: `/Users/pico/run/ss14-wrapper.sock` (filesystem, not a port).

### 2.5 What ruled out earlier approaches (for the file)

- **DNAT (parent's pre-pivot plan)**: exposes home WAN IP via DDNS resolution.
- **TPROXY (`dotfiles-r8k`)**: asymmetric routing — return path requires `tc nat egress` (Linux-only) on the backend; macOS has no equivalent that scales beyond per-client routes (O(connected players)).
- **GRE/IPIP-over-Tailscale (`dotfiles-jjd`)**: Tailscale's WireGuard drops non-TCP/UDP/ICMP IP protocols (upstream issue tailscale/tailscale#14006, unresolved since 2024-11-02). GRE/IPIP-over-public-IP would re-expose pico's home WAN IP.

C2 is the escalation path the parent flagged as "biggest engineering scope but most likely to actually deliver the goal."

## 3. Changes and Decisions

### 3.1 Change — Add an nginx stream block with PROXY-protocol on zig-computer

- **Why**: nginx's `proxy_protocol on` directive prepends a PROXY-protocol header to the outbound stream (TCP or UDP) carrying the original client's IP+port. nginx 1.28 (Ubuntu's questing-shipped stable) supports PROXY-protocol for UDP since nginx 1.27.x. **Empirically verified via /check (dotfiles-9cj) on 2026-05-24: nginx 1.28 emits PROXY v1 (text format), not v2 (binary), on UDP** — both formats convey the same real-client IP+port info; the wrapper consumes either via the `go-proxyproto` library's autodetecting reader. No build flag changes — `--with-stream=dynamic` is already present.
- **How**: New file `/etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-game.conf` (see §4.1).

### 3.2 Change — Introduce a wrapper daemon on pico

- **Why**: Lidgren does not parse PROXY headers (`grep -rn "PROXY\|proxy_protocol\|ProxyProtocol"` in `RobustToolbox/` returns zero hits). Adding parsing INSIDE Lidgren is a fork of the network library, with much larger blast radius than a sidecar daemon. The wrapper isolates PROXY-protocol handling from the game engine, lets Robust.Server believe it's still receiving plain UDP/TCP from `localhost`, and gives us a clean local API for the moderation override.
- **How**: Single-binary daemon written in **Go** (decision below in §3.6) at `~/dotfiles/ss14/wrapper/`. Reads PROXY-protocol headers (v1 text or v2 binary — autodetected via `go-proxyproto` library), forwards stripped payloads to `127.0.0.1:1213`, exposes a session-lookup API over a UDS.

### 3.3 Change — Patch vendored Robust.Server in two places to consult the wrapper API

- **Why**: The cleanest minimum-surface override. Every IP-reading site downstream funnels through one of:
  - `INetChannel.RemoteEndPoint` (NetChannel.cs:40)
  - `NetConnectingArgs.IP` (synthesized in NetManager.ServerAuth.cs:231)
  Override these two and every Content.Server moderation read becomes honest with zero changes in Content.Server itself.
- **How**: Inject a small `IRemoteAddressOverride` service into NetManager; service is implemented by a class that talks to the wrapper's UDS API. Default (no-wrapper-running) behavior: pass-through — returns `_connection.RemoteEndPoint` unchanged. Fork-fail-safe.

### 3.4 Change — LaunchAgent for the wrapper

- **Why**: All other persistent pico services follow the `~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.<service>.plist` pattern (gotcha #15 — never use `brew services`, write our own plist with envs baked in).
- **How**: `~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.ss14-wrapper.plist`, KeepAlive=true, `RunAtLoad=true`, `EnvironmentVariables` includes `SS14_WRAPPER_LISTEN=$(tailscale ip -4):1212`, `SS14_WRAPPER_UPSTREAM=127.0.0.1:1213`, `SS14_WRAPPER_SOCK=/Users/pico/run/ss14-wrapper.sock`. Template sed-substitutes `TAILSCALE_IP_PLACEHOLDER` at install time (per Phase 2 Ollama plist precedent).

### 3.5 Change — ss14-watchdog config flip to bind 1213, not 1212

- **Why**: The wrapper owns `:1212` on pico's tailnet IP; Robust.Server moves to `127.0.0.1:1213`.
- **How**: Patch the instance's `config.toml` with `[net] port = 1213` and `bindto = "127.0.0.1"`. Update `~/dotfiles/vs14/` templates accordingly. Also patch `status.connectaddress` to remain pointing at the **public hostname** `ss14.zig.computer:1212` (NOT pico's tailnet IP — the launcher must reach the public face).

### 3.6 Decision — Wrapper language is Go

- **Tradeoffs considered**:
  - **Go** (chosen): single static binary, excellent stdlib for net/UDP + Unix sockets, established PROXY-protocol library at `github.com/pires/go-proxyproto`, cross-compiles cleanly to darwin-arm64, no runtime install. Builds in `dotnet`-installer-free CI. Familiar to the user (reef-router is bun/TS, but ops tooling on pico is comfortable Go-or-anything).
  - **Rust** rejected: more dev-time for marginal runtime gains; UDP-forward + UDS-server is solved-problem territory where Go's stdlib is genuinely good enough.
  - **Python** rejected: GIL + GC pauses on a UDP-hot-path are risk. Phase 4 already observed `MsgEntity Diff: -4250` mystery disconnects we couldn't fully isolate; the wrapper should add zero new GC sources.
  - **C#** rejected: another whole dotnet runtime on the box for one process, AND a managed sidecar to a managed server doesn't simplify the deployment story.

### 3.7 Decision — Wrapper API is UDS (not HTTP)

- **Tradeoffs**:
  - **UDS** (chosen): no port allocation, no firewall question, no localhost-binding question, sub-millisecond round-trip, dirt-simple permission model (filesystem mode 0660 + group). Robust.Server already runs as `pico:staff`; wrapper plist drops privileges to same user; both can read/write the socket.
  - **HTTP loopback**: simpler to debug (`curl http://127.0.0.1:NNNN/lookup/...`), but requires a port allocation and the cost is the round-trip latency that matters on **every UDP datagram in the connect handshake**. With UDP at 30 Hz + ~50ms ban-check budget, even 1ms HTTP overhead × N calls during a connect storm adds up.
  - **In-memory shared file** (mmap): pure-speed winner but the synchronization design surface (multi-process atomic writes) is non-trivial; UDS is good enough and the protocol is much simpler to evolve.

### 3.8 Decision — Session key is `(client-5-tuple)` keyed by Lidgren's view

- **Why this matters**: Lidgren multiplexes many "Net peers" over the single UDP socket. The wrapper has to map "this incoming UDP packet" to "this real client IP." nginx provides the PROXY header ONCE per stream (TCP) or **on EVERY UDP datagram** of a "UDP session" (nginx's term — keyed on client 5-tuple with a configurable timeout; per-datagram header behavior verified empirically via /check dotfiles-9cj 2026-05-24). After header strip, the payload reaches the wrapper and gets forwarded to Robust.Server bearing the wrapper's loopback source.
- **The mapping** the wrapper maintains: `(wrapper-loopback-port-it-just-allocated, Robust.Server-port-1213)` → real client IP. Robust.Server, when it wants to know "who is this peer?", asks the wrapper API: `LOOKUP <5-tuple-as-seen-by-Robust>` and the wrapper answers with the real client IP.
- **Why this works for the multiplex problem**: nginx stream's `proxy_upload_rate`/UDP-session model already keys by client 5-tuple. The wrapper inherits that key. For each unique (client-5-tuple), the wrapper allocates a unique outbound source port (call it the "fan-out port"), forwards to Robust.Server, and Robust.Server sees `127.0.0.1:<fan-out-port>` as the peer. Lookup by that 5-tuple gives the real client.
- **Wrapper is stateless across restarts** (ratified by /check dotfiles-9cj on OQ-02): no session-map persistence to disk; each wrapper process starts with an empty map and re-populates lazily from incoming per-datagram PROXY headers. Wrapper restart costs every active player a Lidgren `ConnectionTimeout`-driven reconnect window (~25s with default settings; see OQ-02).

### 3.9 Decision — Failure semantics: wrapper crash leaves Robust.Server up but moderation becomes pass-through

- **Why**: Robust.Server must not crash because the wrapper crashed. If the IRemoteAddressOverride service's UDS connect fails, it must fall back to `_connection.RemoteEndPoint` (the proxy's IP) and continue serving. Moderation degrades to "every player looks like 127.0.0.1" for the duration. The wrapper's LaunchAgent KeepAlive will restart the wrapper within ~1 sec. Players already connected see no interruption; new connects during the gap have wrong IPs in `ban_address` etc.
- **Counter-design considered**: hard-fail Robust.Server on UDS connect error. Rejected because (a) Phase 4's rollback was driven partly by avoiding "single point breaks the game"; (b) cached moderation data already loaded covers most cases; (c) the alert path can be a log-only warning that the orchestrator's o11y stack picks up.

### 3.10 Decision — vacation-station-14 patches live as a maintained fork patch set, not an upstream PR

- **Why**: Parent bead explicitly says "The fork is OK to maintain (vacation-station-14 is already a community fork; no upstream-PR pressure)." Upstream Space Wizards' production hosting model (per `space-wizards/docs` referenced in zig-zone runbook) explicitly tells operators NOT to reverse-proxy UDP; they're highly unlikely to merge.
- **How**: Patches land on the `main` branch of vacation-station-14 with a `Bead: dotfiles-NNNN` trailer that traces back here. If upstream ever adds a similar feature, we revert the patches and consume theirs. Until then, FORK_POINT (existing file in `~/vacation-station-14/FORK_POINT`) gets a new line noting the SS14-on-pico patch series.

## 4. Formal Specification

### 4.1 nginx stream config (zig-computer)

`/etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-game.conf` (REPLACES any prior empty file; created NEW):

```nginx
# SS14 game traffic — forwards to pico with PROXY protocol (v1 text on UDP, per /check)
# carrying real client IP. Approach C2 per spec dotfiles-9g1. Replaces direct localhost
# binding (Phase 4 rolled back).

upstream ss14_pico_udp {
    server 100.95.4.73:1212;
    # tailnet IP only — pico is NOT publicly routable.
}

upstream ss14_pico_tcp {
    server 100.95.4.73:1212;
}

server {
    listen 1212 udp reuseport;
    proxy_pass ss14_pico_udp;
    proxy_protocol on;          # PROXY v1 text header prepended to EACH UDP datagram
                                # (verified empirically /check dotfiles-9cj 2026-05-24;
                                #  nginx 1.28 emits v1, not v2, on UDP — wrapper autodetects)
    proxy_responses 1;           # Lidgren handshake is stateful — DO NOT set to 0 (gotcha #23)
    proxy_timeout 120s;          # 2-minute window — comfortably exceeds Lidgren's 1Hz keepalive
                                 # (Robust overrides Lidgren PingInterval=1s at
                                 #  RobustToolbox/Robust.Shared/Network/NetManager.cs:655),
                                 # with headroom for transient packet loss / NAT rebinding.
                                 # nginx default for UDP proxy_timeout is 600s; 120s is the
                                 # tightened production setting chosen via /check (dotfiles-9cj).
    proxy_buffer_size 16k;       # accommodate larger Lidgren packets
}

server {
    listen 1212;                 # TCP — used for /info status endpoint + initial handshake
    proxy_pass ss14_pico_tcp;
    proxy_protocol on;
    proxy_timeout 30s;
}
```

### 4.2 Wrapper daemon (Go, single binary)

Project layout: `~/dotfiles/ss14/wrapper/`
```
ss14-wrapper/
├── go.mod
├── main.go
├── proxy.go        # UDP+TCP listener, PROXY-protocol parse, forward
├── api.go          # UDS server: LOOKUP, ENUMERATE, HEALTH
├── session.go      # 5-tuple session map with TTL cleanup
└── README.md
```

Pseudocode for the hot path (proxy.go, UDP):

```go
// sketch
func ServeUDP(listenAddr, upstreamAddr string, store *SessionStore) error {
    listener, _ := net.ListenPacket("udp", listenAddr)  // bind tailnet-IP:1212
    upstream, _ := net.ResolveUDPAddr("udp", upstreamAddr)  // 127.0.0.1:1213
    buf := make([]byte, 65535)
    for {
        n, srcAddr, _ := listener.ReadFrom(buf)
        // nginx prepends a PROXY-protocol header to EVERY UDP datagram
        // (verified /check dotfiles-9cj — nginx 1.28 emits v1 text format on UDP,
        // per-datagram, NOT once-per-session as a v1 of this spec assumed).
        // The go-proxyproto library's autodetecting Reader handles v1 and v2
        // transparently — we parse on every datagram.
        reader := bytes.NewReader(buf[:n])
        hdr, err := proxyproto.Read(bufio.NewReader(reader))  // autodetects v1/v2
        if err != nil { /* malformed header — log + drop, see TC18 */ continue }
        realSrc := hdr.SourceAddr  // net.Addr — *net.TCPAddr in practice (v1 only labels TCP4/TCP6)
        payloadOffset := int(reader.Size()) - reader.Len()
        payload := buf[payloadOffset:n]
        // Session-store maps fan-out-port → real-client-IP for the Robust.Server
        // LOOKUP API. LookupOrCreate is keyed by srcAddr (nginx-side 5-tuple);
        // first sight of a 5-tuple allocates a unique loopback fan-out port.
        sess, _ := store.LookupOrCreate(srcAddr, realSrc)
        // Outbound fan-out socket per session (allocated lazily in LookupOrCreate)
        // gives Robust.Server a unique 127.0.0.1:<fan-out-port> per real client.
        _, _ = sess.FanOut.WriteToUDP(payload, upstream)
    }
}

func ServeReturnPath(store *SessionStore, listener net.PacketConn) {
    // Robust.Server's reply lands on the per-session fan-out socket.
    // Pump it back out the public listener with the original src 5-tuple.
    for sess := range store.AllSessions() {
        go func(s *Session) {
            buf := make([]byte, 65535)
            for {
                n, _, _ := s.FanOut.ReadFrom(buf)
                _, _ = listener.WriteTo(buf[:n], s.PublicSrcAddr)
            }
        }(sess)
    }
}
```

The TCP path is simpler (one PROXY header per connection, no per-packet juggling):

```go
// sketch
func ServeTCP(listenAddr, upstreamAddr string, store *SessionStore) error {
    ln, _ := net.Listen("tcp", listenAddr)
    for {
        conn, _ := ln.Accept()
        go handleTCP(conn, upstreamAddr, store)
    }
}
func handleTCP(client net.Conn, upstreamAddr string, store *SessionStore) {
    // PROXY header (v1 text or v2 binary — library autodetects) is first bytes on the wire.
    // Per /check dotfiles-9cj, nginx's default for TCP is v2 binary, but go-proxyproto's
    // generic Reader handles both transparently — no per-format branching needed.
    reader := bufio.NewReader(client)
    hdr, err := proxyproto.Read(reader)
    if err != nil { client.Close(); return }
    realSrc := hdr.SourceAddr  // *net.TCPAddr
    upstream, _ := net.Dial("tcp", upstreamAddr)  // 127.0.0.1:1213
    // Register session keyed by upstream local port for the LOOKUP API.
    store.AddTCPSession(upstream.LocalAddr().(*net.TCPAddr).Port, realSrc)
    defer store.RemoveTCPSession(upstream.LocalAddr().(*net.TCPAddr).Port)
    bidirectionalCopy(reader, upstream)  // 'reader' starts after the consumed PROXY header
}
```

UDS API (api.go):

```
WIRE PROTOCOL (newline-delimited text, request/response):
  Request:   "LOOKUP <proto> <local-port>\n"            // proto = udp|tcp
  Response:  "OK <real-client-ip>:<real-client-port>\n" // or "MISS\n"

  Request:   "ENUMERATE\n"
  Response:  N lines of "<proto> <local-port> <real-ip>:<real-port>\n" then "END\n"

  Request:   "HEALTH\n"
  Response:  "OK active_sessions=N uptime_s=X\n"
```

Why a text protocol: trivially debuggable (`nc -U /Users/pico/run/ss14-wrapper.sock`), no schema-evolution drama, ~zero parse cost vs binary at the volumes involved (one LOOKUP per Robust.Server moderation call, not per packet).

**New interface — `RobustToolbox/Robust.Shared/Network/IRemoteAddressOverride.cs`**:

**Default implementation — `Content.Server/Connection/Ss14WrapperRemoteAddressOverride.cs`** (lives in the content layer to avoid polluting Robust.Shared with content-specific dep):

```csharp
// sketch
public sealed class Ss14WrapperRemoteAddressOverride : IRemoteAddressOverride
{
    private readonly string _sockPath;  // "/Users/pico/run/ss14-wrapper.sock"
    private readonly ISawmill _log;

    public IPEndPoint? Lookup(string protocol, IPEndPoint observed)
    {
        try
        {
            using var sock = new UnixDomainSocketClient(_sockPath);
            sock.Send($"LOOKUP {protocol} {observed.Port}\n");
            var reply = sock.ReceiveLine();
            if (reply.StartsWith("MISS")) return null;
            if (reply.StartsWith("OK ")) return IPEndPoint.Parse(reply[3..].Trim());
            return null;
        }
        catch (Exception ex)
        {
            _log.Warning("ss14-wrapper UDS lookup failed (fallback to raw): {Err}", ex.Message);
            return null;  // pass-through; moderation degrades gracefully (§3.9)
        }
    }
}
```

Registered in `ServerContentIoC.cs` only when `_cfg.GetCVar(CCVars.NetWrapperEnabled)` is true (new server-only ARCHIVE cvar — see §4.7). Default: false. Set true in `config.toml` only on pico.

### 4.6 Cutover playbook (atomic-ish)

Phase order:
1. **(off-hours window)** Confirm no players online: `curl https://ss14.zig.computer/status` shows `players: 0`.
2. **On pico**: build wrapper binary, install to `/Users/pico/bin/ss14-wrapper`. Run sanity: `ss14-wrapper --healthcheck` returns 0.
3. **On pico**: install + load `com.zig.ss14-wrapper.plist`. Verify with `launchctl list | grep ss14-wrapper` (status `0` = running clean).
4. **On pico**: patch `config.toml` to `port = 1213` + bindto `127.0.0.1` + connectaddress `udp://ss14.zig.computer:1212` + `[net.wrapper]` block.
5. **On pico**: load `com.zig.ss14-watchdog.plist`. Verify Robust.Server bound `127.0.0.1:1213`: `lsof -i :1213 -P | grep LISTEN`.
6. **On zig-computer**: write `/etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-game.conf` per §4.1. `sudo nginx -t`.
7. **On zig-computer**: stop existing ss14-watchdog: `sudo systemctl stop ss14-watchdog`. Verify `:1212` free: `ss -tunap | grep :1212` shows nothing.
8. **On zig-computer**: `sudo systemctl reload nginx`. Verify nginx binds `:1212`: `ss -tunap | grep :1212` shows nginx workers.
9. **Verification**: synthetic test from a third tailnet device — `nc -u zig-computer-public-IP 1212 < known-bytes`. tcpdump on pico's wrapper-side confirms PROXY header arrives intact (PROXY v1 text on UDP per /check dotfiles-9cj; PROXY v2 binary on TCP). `nc -U /Users/pico/run/ss14-wrapper.sock` + `ENUMERATE\n` shows the session.
10. **Real-player verification**: connect with SS14 launcher. Issue a test `ban_address` from admin tools. `psql -c "SELECT address FROM ban_address ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 1"` returns the **real** client IP, not zig-computer's tailnet IP. PASS.
11. **On zig-computer**: `sudo systemctl disable ss14-watchdog`. Move `ss14-replay-rotate.timer` off zig-computer; load `com.zig.ss14-replay-rotate.plist` on pico.

Rollback: stop wrapper + watchdog on pico; on zig-computer remove `streams-enabled/ss14-game.conf`, `nginx -s reload`, re-enable+start `ss14-watchdog.service`. Pre-cutover state fully restored.

## 5. Test Cases

### TC01 — PROXY header parse (wrapper unit) — v1 text format AND v2 binary autodetect
INPUT (a): hand-crafted UDP datagram with PROXY v1 text header `PROXY TCP4 203.0.113.5 100.95.4.73 54321 1212\r\n` + 16-byte payload `"ss14-handshake"` (this is the format nginx 1.28 actually emits on UDP, per /check dotfiles-9cj).
INPUT (b): hand-crafted UDP datagram with PROXY v2 binary header (12-byte signature `\x0D\x0A\x0D\x0A\x00\x0D\x0A\x51\x55\x49\x54\x0A` + version/family/length + AF_INET addrs) src `203.0.113.5:54321` + dst `100.95.4.73:1212` + 16-byte payload `"ss14-handshake"`.
EXPECTED (both): the autodetecting `proxyproto.Read(bufio.NewReader(...))` reader returns a `Header` whose `SourceAddr` resolves to `203.0.113.5:54321`, the payload offset equals the header length (47 bytes for v1 input above, 28 bytes for the v2 input), and the stripped payload bytes equal the input bytes after the offset.
RATIONALE: confirms the wrapper correctly peels both header formats (nginx 1.28 sends v1, but the library is autodetecting so a future nginx version switching to v2 still works without code changes).

### TC02 — Wrapper session map: NEW session creates outbound socket
INPUT: First datagram from client `203.0.113.5:54321` arrives at wrapper's listener.
EXPECTED: `SessionStore.LookupOrCreate` returns `isNew=true`, allocates a unique loopback source port, records the mapping (`fan-out-port → 203.0.113.5:54321`).
RATIONALE: confirms unique fan-out per real client, so Robust.Server sees distinct peers.

### TC03 — Wrapper session map: SECOND datagram from same 5-tuple reuses session, header re-parsed
INPUT: After TC02, a second datagram from `203.0.113.5:54321` arrives — and ALSO carries a PROXY v1 text header (per /check dotfiles-9cj, nginx 1.28 emits the header on EVERY UDP datagram, not just the first).
EXPECTED: Wrapper re-parses the PROXY header, extracts the same real-client IP `203.0.113.5:54321`, `LookupOrCreate` returns `isNew=false` (same srcAddr as TC02), fan-out port matches TC02's. Stripped payload is forwarded via the same loopback socket.
RATIONALE: confirms session-continuity under the actual nginx UDP behavior (header is per-datagram; the wrapper must tolerate re-seeing it without allocating a new fan-out port). Lidgren multiplexes packets; reordering must not lose state.

### TC04 — Wrapper UDS LOOKUP returns the real IP
INPUT: After TC02, a UDS client sends `LOOKUP udp <fan-out-port>\n`.
EXPECTED: Response `OK 203.0.113.5:54321\n`.
RATIONALE: confirms the API surface Robust.Server's patch will consume.

### TC05 — Wrapper UDS LOOKUP returns MISS for unknown port
INPUT: UDS client sends `LOOKUP udp 9999\n` where no session exists.
EXPECTED: Response `MISS\n`.
RATIONALE: confirms negative-path behavior; ensures Robust.Server falls back to raw endpoint.

### TC06 — End-to-end real-client IP appears in ban_address
INPUT: With full stack running (nginx + wrapper + Robust.Server), an SS14 launcher dials `ss14.zig.computer:1212` from client public IP `198.51.100.99`. Admin issues `/ban 198.51.100.99 testing`.
EXPECTED: `psql -c "SELECT address FROM ban_address ORDER BY id DESC LIMIT 1"` returns `198.51.100.99`.
RATIONALE: the headline acceptance test; the entire spec exists to make this row correct.

### TC07 — Wrapper crash mid-game: Robust.Server stays up, moderation degrades to pass-through
INPUT: Send `kill -9` to the wrapper PID while a player is connected and a fresh connection arrives.
EXPECTED: (a) Robust.Server does not crash. (b) Already-connected player's session continues until next packet — then disconnects when wrapper-routed UDP stops arriving (acceptable; nginx will resume forwarding on wrapper restart). (c) During the gap, any `BanManager.BanMatchesPlayer` call's UDS lookup throws → patch returns `null` → fallback `raw` endpoint (`127.0.0.1:NNNN`) used. (d) Wrapper restarts within ~1 sec via LaunchAgent KeepAlive. (e) Post-restart, NEW connections re-establish with PROXY headers; OLD connections cycle.
RATIONALE: confirms graceful degradation per §3.9.

### TC08 — Wrapper UDP session TTL eviction
INPUT: Open a session via TC02, then wait `proxy_timeout` (120s per §4.1, set per OQ-01 decision) without any traffic.
EXPECTED: Wrapper's session store evicts the entry. A subsequent LOOKUP returns MISS. A new datagram from the same client triggers a fresh PROXY header from nginx + a fresh session in the wrapper.
RATIONALE: confirms session GC matches nginx's UDP session lifetime. Note: a real connected SS14 player generates ~1Hz Lidgren keepalive traffic (Robust overrides Lidgren `PingInterval = 1f` at NetManager.cs:655), so 120s eviction will only fire for genuinely idle sessions (paused-tab / NAT-rebind / lost-connection edge cases).

### TC09 — Concurrent sessions: 10 distinct clients
INPUT: Synthetically replay 10 distinct (src-IP, src-port) flows through the wrapper simultaneously.
EXPECTED: 10 unique fan-out ports allocated; 10 unique entries in `ENUMERATE`; Robust.Server's `RemoteEndPoint` per channel returns 10 distinct real IPs via LOOKUP.
RATIONALE: confirms the multiplexing model handles realistic player counts.

### TC10 — Bypass cvar default: with `net.wrapper_enabled=false`, no UDS calls happen
INPUT: Run Robust.Server with `net.wrapper_enabled=false` (default). Send a datagram.
EXPECTED: `NetChannel.RemoteEndPoint` returns `_connection.RemoteEndPoint` directly; no UDS connect attempted (verified via `strace`/`lsof` on the wrapper socket showing zero connections).
RATIONALE: confirms safe-default — turning off the patch returns to vanilla behavior.

### TC11 — TCP /status endpoint reachable + correct response
INPUT: From a client public IP, `curl http://ss14.zig.computer:1212/status` (TCP).
EXPECTED: HTTP 200, valid JSON `players:` field. With players connected, `players` count reflects them (gotcha #22's `admins_count_in_playercount=true` already in config).
RATIONALE: confirms TCP path through the stream block works; admin tooling that polls `/status` is unaffected.

### TC12 — IPv4-only first ship: connecting client over IPv6 is gracefully refused
INPUT: SS14 launcher dials `ss14.zig.computer:1212` over IPv6 (forced via launcher config).
EXPECTED: Connection fails cleanly OR succeeds via the nginx server listening on IPv4 only — with a clear server log noting "no IPv6 listener." NOT a silent hang or RemoteEndPoint = `::1`.
RATIONALE: IPv6 is out of scope first ship; behavior must be predictable (see §6 OQ-08 for future work).

### TC13 — nginx hot-reload preserves in-flight UDP sessions
INPUT: With wrapper + watchdog running and a real player connected, `sudo systemctl reload nginx` on zig-computer.
EXPECTED: Player's session continues without disconnect (PROXY-header sessions are re-emitted by nginx on next packet of the existing 5-tuple; wrapper either continues using the existing session OR re-creates from the new header — both are OK as long as the player doesn't drop).
RATIONALE: critical for ops; if hot-reload kicks every player, we can't iterate the nginx config.

### TC14 — GeoIP / IPIntel cache reads real IP
INPUT: With `net.wrapper_enabled=true` AND `game.ipintel_enabled=true`, a fresh connection from a known IP `203.0.113.5`. Pre-seed `ipintel_cache` table with `(203.0.113.5, score=0)`.

### TC15 — `AdminAlertIfSharedConnection` correctly identifies real shared IPs
INPUT: Two distinct SS14 clients connect from the SAME real public IP `198.51.100.50` (e.g., siblings sharing home NAT). `CCVars.AdminAlertMinPlayersSharingConnection = 2`.
