### TC16 — Concurrent connect-storm of 30 players
INPUT: 30 synthetic SS14-handshake-like UDP flows arrive within a 5-second window.
EXPECTED: Wrapper allocates 30 fan-out ports without contention; UDS LOOKUP latency p99 < 5ms; no UDP datagrams dropped; Robust.Server's auth pipeline completes for all 30 inside the normal handshake budget.
RATIONALE: load test; confirms the wrapper isn't a bottleneck at typical SS14 server capacity (`net.max_connections = 256` default).

### TC17 — Cutover playbook is fully reversible
INPUT: Execute §4.6 steps 1-11 forward, then execute the rollback flow.
EXPECTED: Post-rollback, `ss -tunap | grep :1212` on zig-computer shows Robust.Server (not nginx) bound; pico's launchctl shows wrapper + watchdog unloaded; nginx config has no stream block for `:1212`; a real client connects successfully to the pre-cutover state.
RATIONALE: ops safety — must be able to revert in one shell session if anything breaks.

### TC18 — PROXY header malformed: wrapper logs + drops, doesn't crash
INPUT: Send a datagram to the wrapper's public listener with the first bytes set to garbage — neither a valid v1 text prefix (`PROXY ` ASCII) nor the v2 binary signature (`\x0D\x0A\x0D\x0A\x00\x0D\x0A\x51\x55\x49\x54\x0A`). The `proxyproto.Read` autodetecting parser must return an error.
EXPECTED: Wrapper increments a "malformed_header" metric, logs at WARN level, drops the packet. Wrapper process stays up. (Both v1 and v2 parse errors handled symmetrically — autodetecting library raises `proxyproto.ErrNoProxyProtocol` or similar; wrapper treats any non-nil error as drop-with-metric.)
RATIONALE: hardening — a malformed header arriving (whether from nginx misconfig or from a probe) must not be a crash vector, and the test must exercise the autodetect failure path for both PROXY format versions.

## 6. Open Questions

Surfaced via a self-interrogator pass after drafting Sections 1-5; severity-ranked P1 (blocks impl) / P2 (impl can proceed with risk) / P3 (nice-to-resolve).

### OQ-01 (P1) — How is the wrapper's UDP "session" key reconciled with Lidgren's NetConnection lifetime? **DECIDED 2026-05-24 via /check (dotfiles-9cj)**
nginx's UDP-session model evicts after `proxy_timeout` (nginx UDP default is 600s, NOT 60s — the spec draft's 60s was a test-deployment value, corrected here). Lidgren's NetConnection has its own lifetime (driven by keepalive + disconnect handshake). If a Lidgren session is still "alive" but nginx evicted the UDP session (e.g., on a paused tab), the next datagram from the client gets a NEW fan-out port at the wrapper — Robust.Server sees it as a NEW peer and starts a fresh handshake, mid-game. Behavior: the client gets disconnected and must reconnect.
- **Resolution**: set nginx `proxy_timeout 120s` (applied in §4.1). Grounded in source: Robust overrides Lidgren `PingInterval = 1f` at `RobustToolbox/Robust.Shared/Network/NetManager.cs:655`, so any connected player generates UDP traffic every ~1s, comfortably under the 120s window with ~2-minute headroom for transient packet loss / NAT rebinding. See `--notes` block on this bead for the full Lidgren-source citation chain.
- **Recommendation (historical, superseded)**: tune `proxy_timeout` to `300s` or higher. Decided down to 120s as the tighter production setting.
- **Affects**: §4.1 nginx config (applied), §4.2 wrapper session store (no change needed), runbook gotcha entry (deferred to zig-zone runbook update bead).

### OQ-02 (P1) — Session lifetime + cleanup semantics on the Robust.Server side **DECIDED 2026-05-24 via /check (dotfiles-9cj)**
When wrapper restarts (TC07), Robust.Server holds NetChannels that point to now-stale 127.0.0.1:NNNN fan-out ports. Does Robust.Server (or Lidgren) correctly detect "peer is gone" within a reasonable timeout (i.e., Lidgren's existing dead-connection detection), or do we leak channels indefinitely?
- **Resolution**: ACCEPT spec §3.9 — rely on Lidgren's existing `ConnectionTimeout` (default 25s; verified at `RobustToolbox/Lidgren.Network/Lidgren.Network/Lidgren.Network/NetPeerConfiguration.cs:123-124`; not overridden server-side). Wrapper is **stateless across restarts** — no session-map persistence to disk; each wrapper process starts empty and re-populates lazily from incoming per-datagram PROXY headers (matches §3.8 as updated). Wrapper restart costs every connected player a ~25s reconnect window via Lidgren's natural dead-peer timeout. Counter-design (wrapper-supplied "session-gone" UDS signal) rejected — more patch surface for marginal improvement; if 25s proves unacceptable post-shipping, add as follow-up. See `--notes` block on this bead for the full reasoning.
- **Recommendation (historical, ratified)**: rely on Lidgren's existing `ConnectionTimeout` — this is now the chosen design.
- **Affects**: §3.9 failure semantics (no change — ratified); §3.8 (added stateless-wrapper note); §4.3 patch surface (no change — no new UDS message types).

### OQ-03 (P1) — Does nginx 1.28 actually emit PROXY v2 on UDP, or only TCP? **DECIDED 2026-05-24 via /check (dotfiles-9cj)**
The nginx changelog confirms `proxy_protocol on` for UDP landed in 1.27.x but the Ubuntu questing-shipped 1.28.0 build's behavior should be verified BEFORE committing. nginx -V shows `--with-stream=dynamic --with-stream_realip_module`, but realip is for INBOUND PROXY (the opposite direction).
- **Resolution**: nginx 1.28 DOES emit PROXY-protocol on UDP — but it's **v1 (text format)**, not v2 (binary). Verified empirically via a synthetic stream config on test port `:11212` forwarding to a Python UDP sniffer at `127.0.0.1:11213` (live SS14 on `:1212` untouched, test config cleanly rolled back). Every datagram arrived with a `PROXY TCP4 <real-src-ip> <dst-ip> <src-port> <dst-port>\r\n` header prepended (NOT the v2 binary signature). Two derived corrections applied to the spec: (a) the wrapper uses `proxyproto.Read(bufio.NewReader(...))` autodetecting reader (handles v1 and v2 transparently) instead of the v2-only `ParseV2`; (b) the header is emitted on EVERY UDP datagram (per-datagram), not "once per UDP session" as the spec draft assumed — see §3.8 and §4.2 updates. Design holds; the C2 architecture is fully validated. See `--notes` block on this bead for the full test method, evidence bytes, and rollback verification.
- **Recommendation (historical, superseded)**: pre-impl synthetic test before committing. Done; corrections applied.
- **Affects**: §3.1 (clarified v1-text), §3.8 (per-datagram header note), §4.1 (proxy_timeout 120s — orthogonal but co-decided), §4.2 (autodetecting reader), TC01 + TC03 + TC18 (v1+v2 autodetect coverage).

### OQ-04 (P2) — Should the IRemoteAddressOverride hook live in RobustToolbox or in Content.Server?
Patching `Robust.Shared/Network/NetManager.NetChannel.cs` introduces a content-layer concern (the IRemoteAddressOverride interface dep) into the engine. Alternatives: (a) Define the interface in Robust.Shared as a totally optional hook (default null = pass-through); (b) Use C#'s `event` mechanism so Content.Server subscribes without changing the engine signature; (c) Subclass NetChannel — but it's `sealed`.
- **Recommendation**: (a) Interface in Robust.Shared, content-layer implementation. Smallest engine diff, minimal coupling. Means a tiny patch to Robust.Shared per impl, but acceptable for a forked engine.
- **Affects**: §3.10 (fork-maintenance burden), §4.3 patch shape.

### OQ-05 (P2) — Wrapper language: confirm Go choice vs Rust
Decision §3.6 picked Go. P2 because the choice is reversible up to the impl-bead start. Risk vector: if a future maintainer is more comfortable in Rust, Go choice adds bus factor.
- **Recommendation**: stay with Go. Rust's safety benefits don't materially matter for a small ~500 LOC UDP-forward + UDS-server; Go's ecosystem for PROXY-protocol parse (`github.com/pires/go-proxyproto`) is mature; cross-compile to darwin-arm64 is one-line.
- **Affects**: §3.6, §4.2 implementation language.

### OQ-06 (P2) — UDS vs HTTP for the wrapper API
Decision §3.7 picked UDS. Risk: UDS-on-macOS sometimes has filesystem-mode subtleties (e.g., parent dir permissions blocking the bind even when the socket file itself is mode-0660). HTTP-loopback is more universal at the cost of port allocation + slightly higher latency.
- **Recommendation**: stay with UDS. The Robust.Server process and the wrapper both run as `pico:staff`; the socket path `/Users/pico/run/` is owner-controlled; modes are predictable. Document the dir-creation step in the LaunchAgent.
- **Affects**: §4.2 UDS path config, §4.4 LaunchAgent (must `mkdir -p /Users/pico/run` before bind).

### OQ-07 (P2) — How does Robust.Server know which (proto, port) to LOOKUP for `NetConnectingArgs.IP` at AUTH time?
At the auth-time site (NetManager.ServerAuth.cs:231), `connection.RemoteEndPoint` is the SAME port the wrapper's fan-out used to forward — i.e., `127.0.0.1:<fan-out-port>`. The wrapper's UDS LOOKUP key is `(udp, fan-out-port)`. So LOOKUP works. But the patch site needs to KNOW the proto (`udp` vs `tcp`); for SS14 the handshake itself is over UDP, but the /status endpoint is TCP and there's a TCP "info" exchange during launcher handshake (pre-game).
- **Recommendation**: NetChannel inherently knows its proto via the underlying NetConnection's socket family. Pass `connection.Socket.ProtocolType` to the Lookup call; route UDS LOOKUP with the correct proto string. Add a fallback that tries `udp` then `tcp` if proto is ambiguous.
- **Affects**: §4.3 patch precision.

### OQ-08 (P2) — IPv6 dual-stack support: when (not if)?
Robust.Server's default `net.bindto = "0.0.0.0,::"` means dual-stack is the expected behavior. The spec ships IPv4 only first. Players who's only path to zig-computer is over IPv6 (rare but real) are excluded.
- **Recommendation**: ship IPv4 first (most production traffic). File a follow-up `task` bead to extend nginx stream + wrapper to handle IPv6: nginx PROXY v2 supports AF_INET6; wrapper needs minor changes to its session-key (use `netip.AddrPort` instead of `*net.UDPAddr` for cleaner v4/v6 handling).
- **Affects**: future work bead (Section 7).

### OQ-09 (P3) — Wrapper performance under sustained 30-Hz tickrate
At 30 ticks/sec × 30 players × ~700-byte packets = ~630 kbit/sec on the wrapper's UDP fan-out path. Trivial throughput; but the wrapper's PER-PACKET cost is one map read + one socket write. Go's `net.PacketConn` may allocate per `ReadFrom` if the buffer isn't reused; verify with `pprof`.
- **Recommendation**: use `sync.Pool` for the read buffer; profile under TC16; document the result in the impl bead.
- **Affects**: implementation detail.

### OQ-10 (P3) — vs14-mapserver coexistence on pico
The mapserver runs in a Colima container on pico (Phase 3). It binds Colima-bridged ports. The wrapper binds tailnet-IP:1212. No port overlap. But: Colima's port-forwarding sometimes flakes (gotcha #20). If wrapper's `:1212` doesn't appear externally because of Colima networking interaction, we'd see "tcpdump on zig-computer shows packets going out, but pico reports nothing arriving."
- **Recommendation**: wrapper binds the tailnet IP DIRECTLY (not through Colima). Verify with `lsof -i :1212 -P` showing the wrapper process, not a Colima `vpnkit` shim. Document the check in the cutover playbook.
- **Affects**: §4.6 cutover step 3 (the `launchctl list` verification).

### OQ-11 (P3) — Connection-establishment timing: PROXY-header round-trip vs SS14 handshake timeout
Lidgren's handshake has multiple round-trips (`MsgLoginStart` → `MsgEncryptionRequest` → `MsgEncryptionResponse` → `MsgLoginSuccess`). The wrapper's UDS LOOKUP happens DURING `NetMgrOnConnecting` event (which is in the middle of `NetManager.ServerAuth.cs`). The UDS round-trip is ~1ms; Lidgren's auth timeout is multiple seconds. Should be a non-issue.
- **Recommendation**: log UDS latency at the patch site; alert if p99 > 10ms; track in impl-bead. No action needed unless it materializes.
- **Affects**: monitoring detail.

### OQ-12 (P3) — Hot-reload of wrapper config without dropping sessions
If we ever need to change the wrapper's upstream port or socket path live, must we stop+start (losing sessions)? Or can we SIGHUP-reload?
- **Recommendation**: no SIGHUP support in v1. Restart loses sessions; players reconnect within Lidgren's timeout (~25s, per OQ-02). Document this as known op limitation.
- **Affects**: future feature.

### OQ-13 (P3) — Telemetry: where does the wrapper publish metrics?
Pico already has a Loki + Prometheus container (Phase 3). The wrapper could expose `/metrics` over HTTP for Prometheus to scrape.
- **Recommendation**: ship without metrics first; add Prometheus exporter as a follow-up bead. The text-protocol UDS already exposes `HEALTH` for spot-checks.
- **Affects**: future work.

## 7. Future Considerations

- **Upstream contribution**: If Space Wizards ever softens their "don't reverse-proxy UDP" stance, the IRemoteAddressOverride interface + the NetChannel hook are the minimal upstream-PR-able piece. The wrapper itself stays out-of-tree as a deployment artifact.
- **IPv6 support**: per OQ-08, file as separate task bead once IPv4 is shipped + stable for a week.
- **Performance scaling**: if SS14 ever pushes past ~256 concurrent connections (current default `net.max_connections`), revisit wrapper's per-session goroutine model — switch to a single epoll-style loop if profiling shows scheduler pressure.
- **Future netcode migration**: If SS14 ever moves off Lidgren (the upstream has occasionally discussed it), this entire wrapper approach becomes moot — the new netcode might natively support PROXY protocol or a similar mechanism. Track upstream RobustToolbox network refactor proposals.
- **Wrapper observability**: Prometheus `/metrics` endpoint exporting `sessions_active`, `lookups_per_sec`, `malformed_headers_total`, `uds_latency_seconds_bucket`. Wire into pico's existing Phase 3 observability stack.
- **DDOS protection at the wrapper layer**: currently inherited from nginx (which has rate-limit primitives) and the tailnet (which has ACL primitives). If real DDOS materializes, consider a `nginx limit_conn` block + wrapper-side connection-rate-limit.
- **Multi-fork compatibility**: if another SS14 community fork wants the same capability, the wrapper + the 2-file patch are extractable as a generic `RobustToolbox-net-wrapper` repo. No vacation-station-14 specifics in the wrapper code (it's just PROXY-protocol → UDS).
- **Re-link to mainline**: when Phase 4 lands per this spec, the zig-zone runbook's Phase 4 section gets rewritten from "ROLLED BACK" to "SHIPPED VIA C2". Two new gotchas likely: (a) nginx UDP PROXY emit version requirement, (b) wrapper-restart implies player-reconnect window. The earlier "definitive architectural argument" against reverse-proxied UDP becomes "definitive UNLESS wrapper-based source-preservation is in play."

notes:
## Decision: OQ-03 — DECIDED 2026-05-24 via /check (dotfiles-9cj)

**Question:** Does nginx 1.28 actually emit PROXY v2 on UDP, or only TCP?

**Verdict:** NEEDS-WORK — nginx 1.28 emits PROXY-protocol on UDP, but it's **v1 (text)** not **v2 (binary)**. Design holds; spec text needs updates.

**Test method:** Synthetic stream config at `/etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-proxyv2-test.conf` (test port :11212 — live SS14 on :1212 untouched). nginx forwarded to a Python UDP sniffer at 127.0.0.1:11213 that dumped raw bytes hex. Three test datagrams sent via `nc -u`:
- From 127.0.0.1 → 127.0.0.1:11212 (loopback)
- From 127.0.0.1 → 100.98.174.21:11212 (tailnet IP dst)
- From 100.98.174.21 → 127.0.0.1:11212 (tailnet IP src, explicit port via `nc -p 55555`)

**Evidence:** Every datagram arrived at sniffer with a textual PROXY v1 header prepended (NOT the v2 binary signature `\x0D\x0A\x0D\x0A\x00\x0D\x0A\x51\x55\x49\x54\x0A`). Example raw bytes:
```
PROXY TCP4 100.98.174.21 127.0.0.1 55555 11212\r\nOQ03-TEST-PAYLOAD-FROMTS
```
Header carries: real-client-IP `100.98.174.21`, real-dst-IP `127.0.0.1`, real-client-port `55555`, real-dst-port `11212`. Source-port pinning verified (explicit `-p 55555` → `55555` in header). The "TCP4" label is a known PROXY-v1 spec limitation (v1 only defines TCP4/TCP6; UDP-over-v1 reuses the TCP4 token per haproxy convention).

**Two assumption corrections vs spec §4.2:**
1. Spec assumed v2 binary header (`proxyproto.ParseV2`). Reality: v1 text. The Go library `github.com/pires/go-proxyproto` auto-detects v1 vs v2 via the first bytes — switching to its generic `Reader.ReadHeader` is a one-line wrapper change. No upstream re-architecture needed.
2. Spec assumed nginx emits header "ONCE per UDP session" (NetManager.NetChannel.cs:40 sketch in §4.2 reads "First packet: parse PROXY v2 header"). Reality: nginx 1.28 emits the header on **every UDP datagram** of a session, not just the first. This actually SIMPLIFIES the wrapper — no per-session header-state needed. The session-store still maps fan-out-port → real-client-IP for the Robust.Server LOOKUP API; the wrapper just parses the header on every UDP datagram and forwards the stripped payload.

**Rollback verified:** YES.
- Test config removed: `sudo rm /etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-proxyv2-test.conf`
- nginx config diff vs pre-snapshot `/tmp/nginx-config-pre.1779650655.txt`: zero changes
- `:11212` no longer bound
- `:1212` still bound to Robust.Server (4 listeners: UDP+TCP × v4+v6) throughout — live SS14 untouched

**Spec impact (re-dispatch /spec to apply):**
- §3.1 — clarify "PROXY-protocol v1 text" instead of "v2 header"
- §4.1 nginx config — `proxy_protocol on` is correct; remove any v2-specific wording
- §4.2 wrapper pseudocode — replace `proxyproto.ParseV2(buf[:n])` with `proxyproto.Read(reader)` (autodetects v1/v2 via library), and remove the "First packet: parse PROXY v2 header" branching since header is per-datagram
- §4.2 SessionStore — no functional change, but the comment "nginx only emits header once per UDP session" is wrong and should be deleted
- TC01 — update to test v1 text-format parse (or both v1 and v2, exercising the library's autodetect)
- TC03 — update INPUT to "second datagram from same 5-tuple ALSO carries a PROXY header" and EXPECTED to "wrapper re-parses, gets same real-client IP, fan-out port unchanged"

---

## Decision: OQ-01 — DECIDED 2026-05-24 via /check (dotfiles-9cj)

**Question:** How is the wrapper's UDP "session" key reconciled with Lidgren's NetConnection lifetime? What `proxy_timeout` setting won't evict active players?

**Decision:** ACCEPT spec recommendation with refinement — set nginx `proxy_timeout 120s` (NOT the spec-draft's 60s, NOT OQ-01's 300s suggestion). Default nginx UDP `proxy_timeout` is actually **10 minutes** (600s) per nginx docs, NOT 60s as the spec text implied.

**Rationale (grounded in source):**
- Lidgren default `PingInterval = 4.0s`, `ConnectionTimeout = 25.0s` (verified at `RobustToolbox/Lidgren.Network/Lidgren.Network/Lidgren.Network/NetPeerConfiguration.cs:123-124`).
- Robust overrides `PingInterval = 1f` (1 second) in `RobustToolbox/Robust.Shared/Network/NetManager.cs:655` — comment: "ping the client once per second."
- Server-side `ConnectionTimeout` is NOT overridden in `NetManager._getBaseNetPeerConfig` (line 683-693 only sets it on the client branch). Server inherits Lidgren default 25s.
- `CVars.ConnectionTimeout` (`net.connection_timeout`, default 25.0s) is flagged `CVar.CLIENTONLY` per `RobustToolbox/Robust.Shared/CVars.cs:305`. So no server-side knob.
- Effective traffic floor for any connected player: 1Hz server→client pings + Lidgren acks. nginx UDP session sees traffic every ~1s minimum.
- Therefore: any `proxy_timeout >= 30s` is safe for active players. `proxy_timeout 120s` gives 2-minute headroom for transient packet loss (mobile clients on flaky wifi, NAT-rebinding pauses) without evicting a still-alive Lidgren peer.

**Refinement vs spec §4.1:** The spec's `proxy_timeout 60s` is OK but `120s` is better. Also: `proxy_responses 1` in §4.1 is CORRECT (Lidgren is request/response stateful, NOT fire-and-forget DNS-style; `proxy_responses 0` would break Lidgren's ack handshake — gotcha #23 already calls this out in the spec).

**Spec impact:**
- §4.1 nginx config — change `proxy_timeout 60s` to `proxy_timeout 120s` on the UDP block. Add a comment line citing Lidgren `PingInterval = 1f` from NetManager.cs:655 as the reason 120s is comfortably wide.
- OQ-01 description text — note that nginx default is 600s (not 60s); the spec's "60s" was the test-deployment value, not nginx's default.

---

## Decision: OQ-02 — DECIDED 2026-05-24 via /check (dotfiles-9cj)

**Question:** When wrapper restarts (TC07), Robust.Server holds NetChannels pointing to now-stale 127.0.0.1:NNNN fan-out ports. Does Lidgren correctly detect "peer is gone" within reasonable timeout, or do we leak channels?

**Decision:** ACCEPT spec §3.9 — rely on Lidgren's existing `ConnectionTimeout = 25s` for natural eviction. Wrapper is **stateless across restarts** (no session persistence). Document the ~25s reconnect window in the runbook as a known op cost of any wrapper restart.

**Rationale:**
- Server-side Lidgren detects dead peers when no inbound traffic arrives for `ConnectionTimeout = 25s` (verified above). With `PingInterval = 1s`, the server sends 25 pings before declaring death — each ping that fails to receive an ack contributes to the timeout countdown.
- Wrapper restart sequence: (a) wrapper crash → session-map lost; (b) LaunchAgent KeepAlive restarts wrapper within ~1s; (c) Robust.Server's outbound pings to old fan-out port go to wrapper, which has no mapping → wrapper drops them (no real-client target); (d) ~25s later, server marks peer dead, closes NetChannel; (e) any active player reconnects via launcher, gets a fresh PROXY-header packet → new session.
- Total worst-case player-side disruption: ~25s + reconnect latency. Acceptable for an unplanned restart; planned restarts can be off-hours.
- Counter-design ("session-gone" UDS signal from wrapper at startup, telling Robust.Server to drop all NetChannels) was considered and rejected:
  - More patch surface (new UDS message type, new server-side handler that walks NetChannels and closes them).
  - More interop coupling (wrapper has to enumerate "active sessions it knows about" and signal them; but the wrapper post-restart has NO knowledge of what was active pre-restart — the only honest signal is "I just started, my map is empty, please drop everything").
  - The 25s natural timeout already provides graceful degradation per spec §3.9.
- If post-shipping we find 25s is too long (e.g., LaunchAgent flapping causes player rage-quits), we can add the "session-gone" signal in a follow-up impl bead. v1 ships without it.

**Wrapper state-management decision: STATELESS.** No persistence of session-map to disk on shutdown. No restoration on startup. Each wrapper process starts with an empty map; populates lazily from incoming PROXY headers. Matches the spec's §3.9 graceful-degradation model.

**Spec impact:**
- §3.9 — no change; this decision RATIFIES the existing design.
- Runbook (zig-zone Phase 4 section, when migrated to SHIPPED via C2): add a gotcha entry "wrapper restart costs ~25s player reconnect window per Lidgren ConnectionTimeout default."
- TC07 EXPECTED text already aligns; no test-case change.

---

title: ssh: Host alias for pico — fixes plain ssh/scp/rsync over tailnet
description:
## Context

Plain `ssh pico` / `scp pico:` failed in mid-A1111-install with `Host key
verification failed.` Re-diagnosis revealed two stacked issues:

1. OpenSSH first-connect prompt has no TTY in non-interactive shells →
   `Host key verification failed.`
2. After accepting the key, OpenSSH defaults `User=$LOGNAME` (e.g. `ubuntu`
   on zig-computer); pico has no `ubuntu` user → tailscaled rejects with
   `failed to look up local user "ubuntu"`.

Originally documented as gotcha #17 in zig-zone runbook (incorrectly
framed as host-key-only). This bead captures the proper fix.

## Steps to Reproduce

On zig-computer (or any tailnet member that doesn't have a `Host pico`
alias and whose local username isn't `pico`):

1. `ssh pico` → `Host key verification failed.` (non-interactive shell
   has no TTY for the first-connect prompt)
2. `ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new pico` → first-connect passes,
   then `tailscale: failed to look up local user "ubuntu"` →
   `Connection closed by 100.x.y.z port 22` (pico has no ubuntu user)
3. Workaround that always works: `ssh pico@pico` — explicit user bypasses
   the second issue, accept-new bypasses the first

## Fix

Host alias in `~/.ssh/local` (gitignored, sourced by `Include local` in
committed `~/dotfiles/ssh/config`):

```
Host pico
    HostName pico.tailfb4637.ts.net
    User pico
    StrictHostKeyChecking accept-new
```

The committed `~/dotfiles/ssh/config` keeps no host-specific data — also
moved the pre-existing gpu block (OVH VPS IP) out to `~/.ssh/local` for
the same reason. The committed file now has just `Include local` plus a
convention comment pointing future-you at the pattern.

## Regression guard

- Convention comment in committed `~/dotfiles/ssh/config` is the structural
  guard — future agents reading the file see "per-host config lives in
  ~/.ssh/local."
- gotcha #17 in zig-zone runbook rewrite is the procedural guard — when
  the symptom resurfaces (fresh dotfiles install on a new machine that
  needs pico access), the gotcha tells you what to add and why.

## Acceptance criteria

- [x] Plain `ssh pico` works (no @user, no flags)
- [x] Plain `scp pico:` works
- [x] No tailnet FQDN / private IPs committed to dotfiles
- [x] gpu block moved out of committed dotfiles (Include-local pattern)
- [x] zig-zone runbook gotcha #17 rewritten with correct root cause
- [x] Comment in `~/dotfiles/ssh/config` documents the convention

title: hooks: add post-gen-fanout-check.sh — gamma/openrouter fan-out reminder
description:
## Context
/gamma and /openrouter are cost-aware skills — one generation per call,
confirm cost first, never fan out variants on speculation. That
discipline is text-only. The "never fire autonomously" half depends on
user intent (unobservable to a hook), but the speculative-loop pattern
— several generations in quick succession — IS mechanically visible.

## Task
Add post-gen-fanout-check.sh, a PostToolUse:Bash hook. It counts
successful gamma-render.sh / openrouter-image.sh invocations per session
in a rolling 10-minute window and prints a one-line stderr reminder on
the 2nd+. WARN only — never blocks (the call already ran; the user may
legitimately want it). Wire it into settings.json.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] post-gen-fanout-check.sh created; PostToolUse:Bash, exits 0 always
- [ ] 2nd+ generation in the window prints a reminder; the 1st does not
- [ ] failed generations (exit_code != 0) not counted
- [ ] wired into settings.json PostToolUse Bash array
- [ ] test-post-gen-fanout-check.sh green

- [ ] post-gen-fanout-check.sh: PostToolUse:Bash, WARN-only, exits 0 always

title: onboard: promote skill-loading to its own gated step
description:
## Context
When a user invokes /onboard, the agent unreliably reads all skill
bodies — it skips some, or delegates the read to an Explore agent whose
context is then discarded. The user has had to explicitly say "read all
skills directly, no Explore agent" every session. The skill already

title: tailnet acl: add Taildrop file-sharing grant (bi-directional server↔user)
description:
## Context

Discovered while delivering `stable-diffusion-handoff.md` to the iPhone
via `tailscale file cp /tmp/stable-diffusion-handoff.md iphone-15-pro:`:
the command refused with `cannot send files: peer is owned by a
different user`. Tailscale's default-deny ACL treats Taildrop as a
per-user feature — devices owned by the same user can drop to each
other, but cross-owner (and tag→user) transfers are blocked by default.

zig-computer + pico are tagged `tag:server` (machine identities, no
user). iPhone + metis are owned by `azigler@`. Without a grant, no
file-sharing between them.

## Fix

Added a `grants` block to `tailscale/acl.jsonc` using the
`tailscale.com/cap/file-sharing` capability. Combined src+dst arrays
cover the full server↔user matrix in one rule. Capability-only grant
(no L3 traffic implication beyond what existing acls allow).

After applying via Tailscale admin console paste:
```bash
tailscale file cp <file> iphone-15-pro:    # tag:server → user
tailscale file cp <file> zig-computer:     # user → tag:server (from metis / iPhone)
```

## Acceptance criteria

- [x] `tailscale/acl.jsonc` updated with `grants` block
- [x] Comment block documents the rationale + capability syntax
- [ ] User pastes updated acl.jsonc into Tailscale admin console
- [ ] `tailscale file cp /tmp/stable-diffusion-handoff.md iphone-15-pro:`
      succeeds from zig-computer (verification target)
- [ ] Inverse direction tested optionally (drop from iPhone Tailscale
      app to zig-computer)

title: zig-zone Phase 4: SS14 game server + watchdog → pico (with feasibility test)
acceptance_criteria:
- [x] SS14 game server runs natively on pico ARM64 (no qemu)
 - [x] SS14.Watchdog runs natively on pico ARM64
 - [x] Client connects through nginx UDP+TCP stream proxy on zig-computer
 - [x] tcpdump verified: client never sees pico's tailnet IP
 - [x] Gameplay works (player tested, admin commands work, postgres writes work)
 - [x] ss14-watchdog systemd unit disabled on zig-computer
 - [x] ss14-replay-rotate timer moved to pico (8th of 8)
 - [x] zig-computer nginx simplified to single proxy / → pico:8080
 - [x] /instances/, /watchdog/, /client.zip routes added to pico nginx
notes:
REVISED CONCLUSION 2026-05-24: SS14 ARM64 build technically works (validated end-to-end with a real player connection), but the deployment model is fundamentally incompatible with reverse-proxied UDP. Per the canonical SS14 architecture (dotfiles-ier research), the game server must live with a public IP that the launcher dials UDP directly. nginx stream{} proxying inverts what Lidgren expects of UDP source addresses. Two intrinsic bugs surfaced: (A) player counter pinned at 0/30 because every client appears as zig-computer's tailnet IP; (B) Lidgren disconnects from Late MsgEntity messages because nginx demuxes UDP into the wrong upstream socket.

ROLLBACK SHIPPED: SS14 game server + watchdog + ss14-replay-rotate timer restored to zig-computer. ARM64 build artifacts preserved on pico (/Users/pico/ss14-watchdog/) in case a future rearchitecture (home-router DNAT for direct UDP, TPROXY, or VPN-based source-preservation) makes SS14-on-pico viable.

Net Phase 4 outcome: SS14 + ss14-watchdog + ss14-replay-rotate STAY on zig-computer permanently. This is a single intentional exception to 'all hostable moves to pico' — justified because zig-computer naturally owns the public IPv4 and SS14's game-server architecture requires that co-location. M1 Max's compute advantages don't help SS14 (latency-bound, not GPU-bound); they DO help the 99% of compute (postgres, LLM, web, builds, obs) which IS on pico.

Spec's Phase 4 wording was sound — the feasibility test result was 'fail'; the spec correctly said 'abort SS14 migration. SS14 stays on zig-computer.'

title: ss14 player count stuck at 0 behind nginx UDP reverse proxy (Lidgren source-IP collapse)
description:
## Context
zig-zone Phase 4 (dotfiles-hdo) moved SS14 game server + watchdog to pico (Mac Studio M1 Max), reached from the public IP via `nginx stream{}` UDP+TCP proxy on zig-computer. Game traffic on `:1212` (Lidgren netcode UDP + TCP /info). The proxy chain:

```
Client (172.x.x.x:50870) →UDP→ zig-computer (51.81.33.136:1212, nginx stream)
                          →UDP→ tailnet (100.98.174.21:39088 → 100.72.47.4:1212)
                          →UDP→ pico Robust.Server (*:1212)
```

Verified working with tcpdump — client's real IP is hidden behind nginx; from pico's perspective every UDP packet arrives with source `100.98.174.21:<ephemeral>` (zig-computer tailnet IP).

## Bugs (two — likely shared root cause)

### Bug A: Player counter shows 0/30 in launcher even with active players
SS14 launcher's server browser polls TCP `/info` which reports `players: 0/30` while actual `spacezig` user is connected and walking around in-game. Connection counter never increments. Admin commands work; postgres writes work; game state syncs.

### Bug B: Random disconnects with "Late MsgEntity! Diff: -4250" warnings
Server logs show:
```
[INFO] net: Approved "100.98.174.21:52905" with username "spacezig"
[WARN] net.ent: Got late MsgEntity! Diff: -4250, msgT: 2102, cT: 6352, player: spacezig
```
Diff is in milliseconds — packets arriving 4+ seconds late. Client times out and disconnects after enough late messages.

## Probable shared root cause
nginx UDP stream proxy collapses ALL upstream UDP into a single "session" keyed by client source IP+port from the proxy's perspective. From Robust.Server's view, every packet looks like it comes from `100.98.174.21` — players never get distinguishable per-IP tracking. Likely Robust.Server:
- Uses source IP+port as the player identity, so multiple players collide
- Counts unique source IPs (proxy-IP-aware code excludes? probably not)
- Reorders / dedupes packets in ways that mismatch Lidgren's expected stream

## Research the subagent should do
The user says: "we are likely not the only SS14 server behind a reverse proxy, so this filtering hypothesis might not be real." Spot on — many SS14 community servers run cloudflare / load balancer / k8s in front. Find the canonical solution.

1. **SS14 / RobustToolbox docs**: search `docs.spacestation14.com` + RobustToolbox README/docs for "reverse proxy", "PROXY protocol", "real_ip", "trusted_proxies", "X-Forwarded-For" (over UDP — unlikely but check), "Lidgren NAT". Production SS14 fork operators must have hit this.
2. **GitHub search**: `github.com/space-wizards/space-station-14` + `RobustToolbox` issues + discussions for "behind reverse proxy", "player count 0", "Late MsgEntity proxy", "source IP collapse", "PROXY protocol UDP", "Lidgren proxy".
3. **nginx config patterns**: nginx supports `proxy_protocol` on `stream{}` to forward original client IP as v1/v2 PROXY header. Does Robust.Server's Lidgren transport support parsing PROXY headers? If yes, that's the fix. If not, find what other community SS14 ops have done.
4. **Lidgren-specific options**: Lidgren has cvars for source-port handling. Check `net.tickrate`, `net.maxconnections`, `net.allowed_addresses`, NAT-related options. Maybe there's a "trust X CIDR as source-stripper" option.
5. **Alternative architectures used by other SS14 hosts**: maybe they run nginx-on-pico binding the public IP via wireguard / tailscale subnet route / bgp / something instead of the zig-computer-proxy pattern. If the canonical answer is "don't proxy UDP", document that and we'll architecturally rethink.

## Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Root cause identified for both Bug A (counter 0/30) and Bug B (Late MsgEntity disconnects)
 - [ ] Concrete fix proposed (config change, code patch, architectural shift) with citation
 - [ ] If fix is PROXY protocol: confirmed whether Robust.Server's Lidgren transport supports parsing
 - [ ] If fix is architectural: describe the alternate pattern with pros/cons vs current setup
 - [ ] Report includes at least 3 cited sources (SS14 docs, GitHub issues, community forum posts, etc.)
notes:
FOLLOWUP RESOLUTION (research agent round 2, 2026-05-24): the counter bug was independent of the UDP proxy. Direct quote from Content.Server/GameTicking/GameTicker.StatusShell.cs:47-49 —
  jObject[\"players\"] = _cfg.GetCVar(CCVars.AdminsCountInReportedPlayerCount)
      ? _playerManager.PlayerCount
      : _playerManager.PlayerCount - _adminManager.ActiveAdmins.Count();
cvar admin.admins_count_in_playercount defaults to false (Content.Shared/CCVar/CCVars.Admin.cs:171). spacezig is admin AND only player → 1-1=0. Hub-advert convention per upstream — admins shouldn't inflate their own server's apparent population.

FIX: added [admin] admins_count_in_playercount = true to /opt/ss14-watchdog/instances/vacation-station/config.toml on zig-computer. Robust.Server bounced to pick up.

Two separate issues investigated and resolved:
- Bug B (Late MsgEntity disconnects): real, fixed via SS14 rollback to zig-computer (canonical SS14 architecture: don't UDP-proxy)
- Bug A (counter 0/30): unrelated; admin-subtract default; cvar flip

title: tmux/com.zig.tmux.plist: replace hardcoded 'pico' with HOSTNAME_PLACEHOLDER (fixup)
description:
## Problem
Just-committed `tmux/com.zig.tmux.plist` (commit 7ec840c, bead dotfiles-s4t) has `<string>pico</string>` hardcoded as the tmux session name. `mac.setup.sh` was already updated to do `sed s/HOSTNAME_PLACEHOLDER/$(hostname -s)/` at install time, expecting the placeholder token — but the actual plist file shipped with the wrong literal.

Caused by a tool-use ordering miss: I tried to Edit the plist after the cp without first reading it (the Edit tool requires prior Read of the file). The Edit silently failed; the rest of the commit went through with the original-from-cp content.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Fresh clone dotfiles on a Mac with hostname != "pico"
2. Run `bash mac.setup.sh`
3. `sed s/HOSTNAME_PLACEHOLDER/<hostname>/` finds NO matches in the plist (the placeholder isn't there)
4. Result: `/Library/LaunchAgents/com.zig.tmux.plist` still has session name = "pico" on every Mac that runs the script

## Fix
Replace `<string>pico</string>` with `<string>HOSTNAME_PLACEHOLDER</string>` in `tmux/com.zig.tmux.plist`. `mac.setup.sh` already does the sed substitution correctly.

## Regression guard
Manual visual check on the committed plist. (No automated test exists for dotfile content; this is a one-off pre-commit-discipline miss.)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] `tmux/com.zig.tmux.plist` line 13 reads `<string>HOSTNAME_PLACEHOLDER</string>` not `<string>pico</string>`
- [x] `mac.setup.sh` sed substitution remains correct (it was already right)

title: ss14-gre: test GRE/IP-in-IP tunnel between zig-computer and pico for SS14
description:
## Context

Sub-bead of `dotfiles-52c` (explore: SS14-on-pico via TPROXY or GRE —
preserve source IPs + VPS-IP public face). This bead is the **GRE / IP-
in-IP tunnel** test track. See parent for full rationale.

## Goal

Validate (or rigorously disprove) that an IP-in-IP tunnel between
zig-computer (Linux) and pico (macOS) can deliver SS14 packets to pico
with real client source IPs intact, by encapsulating entire packets
end-to-end through the tunnel. Zig-computer remains the only publicly-
known address.

## Phase 1 research findings (2026-05-24)

- **macOS gif(4) supports IP-in-IP only, NOT GRE.** macOS Sonoma 14.4
  on pico ships gif0 (already present, POINTOPOINT,MULTICAST flag, not
  UP). gre(4) is BSD-specific and not in darwin. So this approach is
  IP-in-IP (proto 4 / IPPROTO_IPV4) end-to-end, not GRE proper. Linux
  side uses `ip tunnel add mode ipip`.
- **Why IP-in-IP solves the asymmetric routing problem TPROXY can't**:
  the tunnel is bidirectional and stateful at the routing layer. Reply
  packets from pico are routed back through gif0 via a /32 route for
  the original client (or, more practically, via an inner-tunnel /30
  with route-rewriting at zig-computer's iptables). Pico does NOT need
  to flip its default route; only specific destinations get the tunnel.
- **Layering on Tailscale**: option A is "carry the tunnel over the
  public IP path direct (proto 4 may be blocked by ISP/firewall on
  one or both ends)"; option B is "carry over tailnet IPs" — meaning
  outer tunnel src/dst are 100.98.174.21 and 100.72.47.4 instead of
  the public IPs. Option B is cleaner because Tailscale's WireGuard
  reliably passes proto 4. **First test uses option B.**
- **No SS14 binary changes needed.** Robust.Server already binds
  to 0.0.0.0; packets arriving at gif0's inner address are
  indistinguishable from packets arriving at any other interface.

## Architecture

Inner tunnel network: `10.99.0.0/30`
- `10.99.0.1` = zig-computer end of tunnel (gre0)
- `10.99.0.2` = pico end of tunnel (gif0)

Outer endpoints (Tailscale-carried for reliability past ISP filters
on protocol 4):
- Outer src zig = 100.98.174.21 (zig-computer tailscale0)
- Outer dst pico = 100.72.47.4 (pico utun5)

Inbound flow (client → game):
1. Client UDP packet hits zig-computer's public IP at :11212
   (src=client-real-IP, dst=51.81.33.136, dport=11212)
2. iptables `mangle PREROUTING` MARKs the packet OR a `DNAT/route`
   redirect rule forwards it into the tunnel without rewriting src.
   The cleanest mechanism is **policy routing**: incoming on ens3
   with dport=11212 → route via gre0 (no NAT). The outer header
   wraps src=tailnet-zig + dst=tailnet-pico; inner header preserves
   original src=client + dst=51.81.33.136.
3. Pico receives outer packet on utun5 (tailnet), kernel decapsulates,
   inner packet appears on gif0. Robust.Server (bound 0.0.0.0:11212)
   accepts it; `RemoteEndPoint.Address` shows client's real IP.

Outbound flow (game → client):
4. Pico's response: src=51.81.33.136 (or 10.99.0.2 — see Open Q's),
   dst=client-real-IP. Routing rule on pico: "for dst=client-real-IP
   (or for src=51.81.33.136), use gif0".
5. Outer wraps src=tailnet-pico + dst=tailnet-zig; arrives at zig-
   computer's gre0.
6. zig-computer iptables `MASQUERADE` or `SNAT` rewrites src to
   51.81.33.136 (if pico responded with 10.99.0.2) and sends out
   ens3 to client.

The asymmetric routing problem dissolves because the tunnel IS the
return path — there is no separate "default route" to mistakenly use.

## Test plan

All tests use port `:11212` (NEVER :1212 which is the live SS14).
Synthetic before real-SS14.

### Step 0 — Snapshot pre-test state

```bash
# zig-computer
sudo iptables-save > /tmp/iptables.pretest.\$(date +%s)
ip rule list > /tmp/iprule.pretest.\$(date +%s)
ip route show table all > /tmp/iproute.pretest.\$(date +%s)
ip tunnel show > /tmp/iptun.pretest.\$(date +%s)
ip addr > /tmp/ipaddr.pretest.\$(date +%s)

# pico
ssh pico 'ifconfig gif0 > /tmp/gif0.pretest 2>&1; netstat -rn > /tmp/route.pretest'
```

### Step 1 — Create IP-in-IP tunnel, zig-computer side (Tailscale-carried)

```bash
# Load ipip module
sudo modprobe ipip

# Create tunnel: outer endpoints = tailnet IPs (proto 4 over WireGuard)
sudo ip tunnel add gre0 mode ipip remote 100.72.47.4 local 100.98.174.21 ttl 64
sudo ip link set gre0 mtu 1400 up
sudo ip addr add 10.99.0.1/30 dev gre0
```

Verify: `ip tunnel show gre0` should list the new tunnel; `ip -4 addr
show gre0` should list 10.99.0.1.

Rollback:
```bash
sudo ip addr del 10.99.0.1/30 dev gre0
sudo ip link set gre0 down
sudo ip tunnel del gre0
# Module stays loaded — harmless
```

### Step 2 — Create gif tunnel, pico side

```bash
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 tunnel 100.72.47.4 100.98.174.21'
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 10.99.0.2 10.99.0.1 netmask 255.255.255.252 up'
ssh pico 'sudo sysctl -w net.inet.ip.forwarding=1'
```

Verify on pico: `ifconfig gif0` shows inet 10.99.0.2 --> 10.99.0.1
and the outer tunnel endpoints. Ping test:
```bash
ping -c 3 10.99.0.2  # from zig-computer
ssh pico 'ping -c 3 10.99.0.1'  # from pico
```

Rollback:
```bash
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 -tunnel'
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 inet 10.99.0.2 delete'
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 down'
ssh pico 'sudo sysctl -w net.inet.ip.forwarding=0'  # IF it was 0 before
```

### Step 3 — Synthetic UDP-echo across the tunnel (NO nginx involved)

This step proves the tunnel works for raw UDP and we can see source
preservation at the tunnel-inner layer. NO production traffic touched.

On pico:
```bash
ssh pico 'nc -u -l 11212' &  # listener bound 0.0.0.0:11212
ssh pico 'sudo tcpdump -ni gif0 port 11212 -c 5' &
```

On zig-computer:
```bash
# Send to pico's tunnel-inner IP (this proves tunnel works at all)
echo "hello-tunnel-\$(date +%s)" | nc -u -q1 10.99.0.2 11212
```

Expected pico tcpdump: `IP 10.99.0.1.<srcport> > 10.99.0.2.11212`
This proves the tunnel carries UDP. Source IP visible at pico = 10.99.0.1
(zig-computer's tunnel-inner). NOT source-preservation yet — that's
the NEXT step.

### Step 4 — Source-preserving forward via policy route (THE actual test)

Now we route INBOUND :11212 traffic from the public interface into
the tunnel WITHOUT rewriting the src IP:

```bash
# Mark inbound :11212 packets
sudo iptables -t mangle -A PREROUTING -i ens3 -p udp --dport 11212 -j MARK --set-mark 0x2

# Policy route marked packets via gre0
sudo ip rule add fwmark 0x2 lookup 200 priority 200
sudo ip route add default dev gre0 table 200

# Allow forwarding for the tunnel (note: sysctl net.ipv4.ip_forward
# is likely already 1 due to docker, but verify)
cat /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward  # expect: 1
```

For pico to reply: routes for the public client IP MUST be set, OR
we use a simpler tactic: send replies back through gre0 by virtue
of the inner-route. If we tell pico's gif0 to be the gateway for ALL
non-LAN, non-tailnet traffic from src 10.99.0.2, we'd hijack pico's
internet outbound. Instead, use a strict per-flow route added on
demand for the test src IP:

```bash
# On pico — only for the synthetic test src
ssh pico 'sudo route -n add -host <synthetic-client-IP> -interface gif0'
```

For the synthetic test, the "client IP" is whatever src we spoof from
zig-computer's nc command. Use `nc -s 10.99.0.1` to send from an
already-tunnel-routed address (no need for a special pico route).

Better test: have nginx do the tunnel-injection. Skip iptables MARK
entirely; configure nginx stream listener on :11212 and proxy_pass
to `10.99.0.2:11212` (the pico tunnel-inner IP). Then verify pico
sees src=10.99.0.1 (zig's tunnel-inner) — that's NOT source-preservation,
it's nginx-rewriting. To get source-preservation, add `proxy_bind
\$remote_addr transparent;` AND the iptables mangle + route rule above.

**Decision: split this into two sub-tests.**

#### Step 4a — Tunnel-only (no source preservation, baseline reachability)

nginx stream config (NEW file, port 11212):
```bash
sudo tee /etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-gre-test.conf <<'CONF'
# TEST ONLY — port 11212; live SS14 stays on :1212 untouched
upstream gre_test_pico {
    server 10.99.0.2:11212;  # pico's tunnel-inner IP
}
server {
    listen 11212 udp;
    proxy_pass gre_test_pico;
    proxy_responses 1;
    proxy_timeout 10s;
}
CONF
sudo nginx -t && sudo systemctl reload nginx
sudo ufw allow 11212/udp comment 'GRE test'
```

Test from off-host (or from zig-computer's localhost — both show that
the data path works):
```bash
echo "ping-via-tunnel" | nc -u -q1 51.81.33.136 11212
ssh pico 'sudo tcpdump -ni gif0 port 11212 -c 5'
# Expected: src=10.99.0.1, dst=10.99.0.2 (nginx-rewritten as expected)
```

Rollback:
```bash
sudo rm /etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-gre-test.conf
sudo nginx -t && sudo systemctl reload nginx
sudo ufw delete allow 11212/udp
```

#### Step 4b — Source-preserving (proxy_bind transparent + tunnel)

This is the END goal. nginx still does the listener, but binds the
forward socket to the client's real src IP. Inside the tunnel, pico
sees src=real-client-IP.

```bash
sudo tee /etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-gre-test.conf <<'CONF'
upstream gre_test_pico {
    server 10.99.0.2:11212;
}
server {
    listen 11212 udp;
    proxy_pass gre_test_pico;
    proxy_bind \$remote_addr transparent;
    proxy_responses 1;
    proxy_timeout 10s;
}
CONF
sudo nginx -t && sudo systemctl reload nginx

# Per nginx 1.13.8+, CAP_NET_RAW is auto-inherited by workers. Verify:
getcap /usr/sbin/nginx || true  # may show cap_net_raw, may need to set
```

For the asymmetric return-path:
```bash
# On pico — route the synthetic test src IP back through gif0
# (only the SPECIFIC client IP, NOT default route)
ssh pico 'sudo route -n add -host <test-client-IP> -interface gif0'
```

Test from a known-distinct src IP (best: from metis tailnet client,
OR a vps that's NOT zig-computer):
```bash
# On the test src:
echo "src-preserve-test" | nc -u -q1 51.81.33.136 11212
```

Expected pico tcpdump on gif0: `IP <test-client-IP>.<sport> >
51.81.33.136.11212` — the **real source IP arrives at pico**. PASS.

Rollback (per-client route on pico):
```bash
ssh pico 'sudo route -n delete -host <test-client-IP>'
```

### Step 5 — Real SS14 client test (deferred until 4b passes)

Only run if 4a + 4b cleanly pass AND we've confirmed live SS14 on
:1212 is unaffected:
```bash
ss -tunap | grep :1212  # should show robust.server still bound
```

Real SS14 client test would require:
- A separate Robust.Server instance on pico bound :11212 (NOT touching
  the prod :1212 on zig-computer)
- pico-side route for the connecting player's IP
- Connect SS14 launcher to `51.81.33.136:11212`
- On pico: tcpdump confirms real IP; psql shows real IP if banned

This is STRETCH GOAL — defer to a follow-up bead if 4b passes.

### Step 6 — Teardown (rollback all steps in reverse)

```bash
# Step 4b rollback (per-host routes on pico, nginx config)
ssh pico 'sudo route -n delete -host <test-client-IP>' 2>/dev/null || true
sudo rm -f /etc/nginx/streams-enabled/ss14-gre-test.conf
sudo nginx -t && sudo systemctl reload nginx
sudo ufw delete allow 11212/udp 2>/dev/null || true

# Step 4a iptables (if applied)
sudo iptables -t mangle -D PREROUTING -i ens3 -p udp --dport 11212 -j MARK --set-mark 0x2 2>/dev/null || true
sudo ip rule del fwmark 0x2 lookup 200 priority 200 2>/dev/null || true
sudo ip route flush table 200 2>/dev/null || true

# Step 2 pico tunnel
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 -tunnel; sudo ifconfig gif0 inet 10.99.0.2 delete; sudo ifconfig gif0 down'
ssh pico 'sudo sysctl -w net.inet.ip.forwarding=0'  # only if it was 0 before

# Step 1 zig tunnel
sudo ip addr del 10.99.0.1/30 dev gre0 2>/dev/null || true
sudo ip link set gre0 down 2>/dev/null || true
sudo ip tunnel del gre0 2>/dev/null || true
```

Verify rollback:
```bash
diff <(sudo iptables-save) /tmp/iptables.pretest.*  # expect empty
diff <(ip rule list) /tmp/iprule.pretest.*  # expect empty
ip tunnel show  # expect empty
ssh pico 'ifconfig gif0' | grep -v "POINTOPOINT,MULTICAST"  # expect baseline only
sudo nginx -t  # expect "test is successful"
ss -tunap | grep :1212  # expect live SS14 still bound
```

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Step-by-step test plan documented (this section)
- [ ] Each step has explicit rollback documented (above)
- [ ] Step 3 (raw tunnel UDP echo) result: PASS / FAIL recorded
- [ ] Step 4a (nginx-via-tunnel, no source preservation) result: PASS / FAIL
- [ ] Step 4b (nginx-via-tunnel + proxy_bind transparent) result:
      pico tcpdump shows real client IP (PASS) or doesn't (FAIL with diagnosis)
- [ ] Real-SS14-client test result (DEFERRED stretch goal)
- [ ] Rollback verified clean against pre-test snapshots
- [ ] Outcome reflected back to parent 52c bead's --notes

## Open questions

- Does the per-client route on pico scale? (For production: every
  connected player's public IP needs a route. A SS14 server with 30
  players = 30 routes added/removed dynamically. Possibly script via
  pf table + a connection-tracking hook. Out of scope for first test.)
- Is there a simpler pf rdr rule on pico that obviates per-client
  routes? (`pass in on gif0 reply-to (utun5 ...)` style — needs
  research.)
- Does Tailscale block protocol-4 inside the WireGuard tunnel? (Likely
  not — WireGuard carries arbitrary IP payloads — but verify with a
  raw `ping -F 4` or by checking tcpdump on utun5.)
- MTU: gif default 1280, tailscale MTU 1280. IPIP adds 20 bytes header.
  Inside-tailnet payload would be 1260. SS14's UDP packets are well
  under 1KB — should be fine. Verify with `ping -s 1200 -M do`.
notes:
## Phase 3 — execution attempt (2026-05-24)

Executed the test plan steps; got to Step 2 (tunnel up on both ends) then
hit a fundamental blocker that aborts the chosen approach. Detail:

### Step 0 — Pre-test snapshot — DONE
- zig-computer: iptables-save (187 lines), ip rule (7), ip route (39),
  ip tunnel (empty), ip addr — all saved to /tmp/*.pretest.1779647689
- pico: ifconfig gif0 (POINTOPOINT,MULTICAST, no inet), netstat -rn,
  net.inet.ip.forwarding=0 — all saved to /tmp/*.pretest.* on pico
- Confirmed live SS14 on :1212: 4 listeners (UDP v4+v6, TCP v4+v6)

### Step 1 — Linux ipip tunnel — DONE
```
sudo modprobe ipip
sudo ip tunnel add gre0 mode ipip remote 100.72.47.4 local 100.98.174.21 ttl 64
sudo ip link set gre0 mtu 1400 up
sudo ip addr add 10.99.0.1/30 dev gre0
```
Result: gre0@NONE UP, link/ipip 100.98.174.21 peer 100.72.47.4,
inet 10.99.0.1/30. Clean.

### Step 2 — macOS gif tunnel — DONE
```
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 tunnel 100.72.47.4 100.98.174.21'
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 10.99.0.2 10.99.0.1 netmask 255.255.255.252 up'
ssh pico 'sudo sysctl -w net.inet.ip.forwarding=1'
```
Result: `gif0: flags=8051<UP,POINTOPOINT,RUNNING,MULTICAST> mtu 1280
	tunnel inet 100.72.47.4 --> 100.98.174.21
	inet 10.99.0.2 --> 10.99.0.1 netmask 0xfffffffc`. Clean.

### Step 2.5 — Connectivity verification — FAIL (DIVERGENCE)

```
ping -c 3 -W 2 10.99.0.2   # from zig-computer
```
Result: **100% packet loss**. tcpdump on pico's utun5 (the tailscale
interface) shows pico IS emitting proto-4 packets:
```
IP 100.72.47.4 > 100.98.174.21: IP 10.99.0.2 > 10.99.0.1: ICMP echo request
```
But tcpdump on zig-computer's tailscale0 shows ONLY TCP/SSH traffic;
**zero proto-4 packets ever arrive at zig-computer**.

### Root cause — Tailscale drops non-TCP/UDP/ICMP IP protocols

The test plan's Open Question #3 ("Does Tailscale block protocol-4
inside the WireGuard tunnel?") is answered: YES, it does. Confirmed by
upstream tailscale issue #14006 "IPIP is not working over tailscale0"
(open since 2024-11-02, unresolved). Tailscale's wireguard
implementation does not relay arbitrary IP protocols; it restricts to
the protocols its userspace stack handles (TCP, UDP, ICMP).

### What this means for the approach

Option A — "carry the tunnel inside Tailscale (outer src/dst = tailnet
IPs)": **BLOCKED by tailscale**. Cannot proceed without an upstream
tailscale fix or a userspace workaround.

Option B — "carry the tunnel over the public IPs (outer src=zig-
computer public, outer dst=pico's home WAN public)": **violates the
core constraint** of this exploration. Pico's home WAN IP would
become the destination of the encapsulated packets — anyone running
tcpdump on the path between zig-computer and pico's home would see
the home WAN IP. Same exposure problem as the rejected DNAT plan.

Option C — "carry the tunnel over a different cloud-relay" (e.g., a
second VPS in the same DC as zig-computer that relays to pico's home
via a non-Tailscale link): defeats the purpose. We already have a
non-Tailscale link (the public internet); the entire point of routing
via zig-computer is that zig-computer's IP is the only one visible.

### Outcome — STOP per "if a step diverges, STOP and report"

Reached the divergence at the connectivity test (Step 2.5, before any
synthetic UDP-echo at Step 3). The architectural premise of "use the
existing tailnet as the tunnel carrier" doesn't hold; the alternatives
either re-introduce the home-IP-exposure problem (option B) or are
escalations beyond the scope of "test in an afternoon" (option C).

### Rollback — DONE and VERIFIED

```
# zig-computer
sudo ip addr del 10.99.0.1/30 dev gre0
sudo ip link set gre0 down
sudo ip tunnel del gre0

# pico
ssh pico 'sudo ifconfig gif0 -tunnel; sudo ifconfig gif0 inet 10.99.0.2 delete; sudo ifconfig gif0 down'
ssh pico 'sudo sysctl -w net.inet.ip.forwarding=0'
```

Verification against pre-test snapshots:
- iptables: diff shows ONLY packet counter changes (expected); no rule changes
- ip rule: empty diff (clean)
- ip route: empty diff (clean)
- ip tunnel: only tunl0 (kernel default, harmless; appeared via modprobe ipip)
- nginx: config test clean (we never touched nginx in this run — short-
  circuited before Step 4)
- pico gif0: tunnel cleared, no inet addrs; flags show
  `POINTOPOINT,RUNNING,MULTICAST` vs baseline `POINTOPOINT,MULTICAST`
  (RUNNING flag is a one-way once-up indicator in BSD; tunnel config is
  fully torn down — confirmed by absence of `tunnel inet ...` lines)
- pico net.inet.ip.forwarding: back to 0 (matches baseline)
- SS14 :1212 live listeners: 4 throughout (no production impact)

### Verdict

**Synthetic UDP-echo: NOT_RUN** (blocked at tunnel-connectivity step)
**Real SS14 client: NOT_RUN**
**Rollback to pre-test: YES, verified**

### Status flip

This bead's PASS/FAIL question is answered "FAIL for the
Tailscale-carried variant; UNTESTED for the bare-public variant
because the bare-public variant requires the very thing we're avoiding
(exposing pico's home WAN IP)". 

Closing this approach as not viable WITHOUT additional infra
investment (e.g., a second cloud relay) OR an upstream Tailscale fix
to issue #14006. Marking the bead as ready for orchestrator review.

Dependencies:
  -> dotfiles-52c (parent-child) - explore: SS14-on-pico via TPROXY or GRE — preserve source IPs + VPS-IP public face

## CLOSED 2026-05-24 — tested, BLOCKED by tailscale#14006

GRE/IPIP-over-Tailscale tested end-to-end. Tunnel comes up clean on
both ends; ICMP/UDP across the tunnel = 100% loss. tcpdump confirms
pico emits proto-4 packets on utun5; zig-computer's tailscale0 sees
zero. Root cause: Tailscale's WireGuard implementation drops non-TCP/
UDP/ICMP IP protocols (upstream tailscale/tailscale#14006, open since
2024-11-02, unresolved as of writing).

Production safety: zero impact on live SS14. All rollback verified.
ipip kernel module loaded as a passive side effect (sink dev tunl0
appeared, harmless).

A non-Tailscale tunnel endpoint (Pi-on-LAN) was considered but doesn't
solve the underlying goal — the Pi→pico hop has the same asymmetric-
routing problem as TPROXY-to-macOS, with no clean fix without making
the Pi pico's default gateway (hurts all outbound).

**Superseded by**: dotfiles-52c continues; the C2 spec bead (opening
next) becomes the live work track. jjd stays closed as a substantive
test result, not a deferral.

title: a1111: upcast_attn insufficient for SDXL inpaint on MPS — escalate to --no-half
description:
## Context

Follow-up to dotfiles-43l (closed earlier today). 43l added
`upcast_attn: true` to config.json — the Settings-only toggle that the
NaN error message itself recommended. The fix was applied as documented
(file installed, LaunchAgent kickstarted, A1111 booted clean), but user
re-tested inpaint immediately after and the NaN still fires:

```
NansException: A tensor with NaNs was produced in Unet. ... Try setting
the "Upcast cross attention layer to float32" option in Settings >
Stable Diffusion or using the --no-half commandline argument to fix this.
```

Verified `~/stable-diffusion-webui/config.json` contains
`{"upcast_attn": true}` and the file is at the correct location. The
setting key matches A1111 source (`shared_options.py:182`). Despite all
that, the NaN check still trips on inpainting attempts. Likely cause:
on MPS, SDXL inpainting has additional fp16 underflow paths beyond the
cross-attention layer that the upcast doesn't cover.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. A1111 on pico per rp0 + 43l (config.json has `upcast_attn: true`)
2. Load SDXL base 1.0
3. Open Img2Img → Inpaint
4. Upload image, draw mask, generate (any prompt, default settings)
5. After ~14s, NansException fires

Repeatable: user has seen it on multiple consecutive inpaint attempts
since 43l's fix.

## Fix

Escalate from "upcast just the cross-attention layer" to "--no-half"
(full fp32 throughout the model). The error message lists this as the
alternative recommended remedy. Trade-off: SDXL memory footprint
doubles from ~6.5GB to ~13GB — fine on M1 Max with 64GB RAM.

Changes to `~/dotfiles/a1111/webui-user.sh`:
- Drop `--upcast-sampling` ("no effect with --no-half" per A1111's
  own help text)
- Add `--no-half`
- Keep `--no-half-vae` (was already there; redundant under --no-half
  but harmless)
- Keep `upcast_attn: true` in config.json as belt-and-suspenders;
  technically moot but doesn't hurt

## Regression guard

- Structural: webui-user.sh in dotfiles carries --no-half as part of
  COMMANDLINE_ARGS. Fresh-Mac rebuild (mac.setup.sh) installs the
  current webui-user.sh, so the fix is durable.
- Documentation: long-form comment in webui-user.sh explains WHY
  --no-half over --upcast-sampling (because the lighter path tested
  insufficient for SDXL inpaint on MPS in this real-world test).

## Acceptance criteria

- [x] webui-user.sh updated with --no-half
- [x] Pico's live webui-user.sh synced
- [x] LaunchAgent kickstarted, A1111 boots clean with new flag
- [ ] User re-tests inpaint, confirms NaN no longer fires
- [ ] Performance impact (memory + iteration speed) acceptable
notes:
## CLOSED 2026-05-24 — verified by user

User retested SDXL inpaint after --no-half landed; inpaint works, no
NaN. Performance impact deemed acceptable in the field (no complaint
on speed/memory; M1 Max 64GB carries the doubled SDXL footprint
without strain).

title: hooks: harden pre-bead-close.sh — worktree guard + scrutiny gate
description:
## Context
"Subagents never run br close / br update --status; bead lifecycle is
orchestrator-only" is repeated across AGENTS.md, /beads, /orchestrator,
/handoff — repeated that often because it gets broken. A subagent
finishing work has the bead ID and br on PATH; the .beads/ symlink
makes a stray close mutate the orchestrator's shared state immediately.
Separately, /scrutinize says an -t impl bead closes only on a SHIP
verdict, but nothing enforces it.

## Task
Add two guards to pre-bead-close.sh (PreToolUse:Bash):
1. Worktree guard — when cwd is inside .claude/worktrees/agent-*, block
   `br close` and `br update --status` (still allow `br update --notes`,
   which /beads explicitly permits subagents to do).
2. Scrutiny gate — closing a `-t impl` bead is blocked unless its
   br-show output carries a SHIP (or OVERRIDE) scrutiny verdict.
Preserve the existing lint gate and chained-cd handling.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Worktree cwd blocks br close + br update --status; allows br update --notes
- [ ] Non-worktree cwd unaffected (orchestrator runs lifecycle normally)
- [ ] -t impl bead with no SHIP/OVERRIDE verdict blocked; with one, allowed
- [ ] Existing lint gate + chained-cd behaviour preserved
- [ ] test-pre-bead-close.sh extended and green
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] worktree guard: blocks br close + br update --status, allows br update --notes
 - [ ] scrutiny gate: -t impl beads need a SHIP/OVERRIDE verdict to close
 - [ ] existing lint + chained-cd behaviour preserved; test green
